<lesson>
  <context>This lesson tackles one of the most important distinctions in Spanish: knowing when to use the preterite versus the imperfect. Both tenses describe the past, but they signal very different things. You'll build a reliable framework for choosing between them, discover how switching tenses can completely change a verb's meaning, and practise applying both tenses together in extended, authentic contexts.</context>

  <section name="Context Clues and Tense Signals">

    <unit type="explanation" name="How to Choose: Preterite or Imperfect?">Explain the core framework for choosing between preterite and imperfect in Spanish. Organise the explanation around five clear patterns, each with paired example sentences showing the same verb in both tenses:

1. Completed/bounded actions (preterite) vs. ongoing, habitual, or background states (imperfect) — e.g. "Ayer comí una pizza." vs. "De niño comía pizza todos los viernes."
2. Preterite time markers: ayer, el lunes pasado, de repente, en ese momento, una vez, finalmente, entonces, aquel día — these signal a specific, finished action.
3. Imperfect time markers: siempre, todos los días, normalmente, generalmente, mientras, de niño/de pequeño, cada verano, a veces — these signal habits, routines, and ongoing past states.
4. The interrupted action pattern: imperfect sets the background scene, preterite interrupts it — e.g. "Leía un libro cuando sonó el teléfono." Explain that "cuando" often signals this pattern.
5. Descriptions and mental/physical states in the past (imperfect) vs. a specific event happening (preterite) — e.g. "Era un día lluvioso." vs. "Llovió toda la tarde."

Keep the tone practical. The student already knows both conjugations — this explanation is about decision-making, not form review.</unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Catching Up After the Holidays">Generate a single conversation between two friends, Elena and Marco, catching up after the summer holidays. Elena talks about specific things she did on her trip (completed events — preterite): arriving, visiting a place, meeting someone, eating something specific. Marco recalls what holidays used to be like when they were children (habitual/descriptive — imperfect): what they always did, what the place was like, how they felt. The conversation should feel natural, not like a grammar lesson. 10–12 turns. The contrast between Elena's preterite-heavy account and Marco's imperfect-heavy memories should emerge organically from the topic, not through forced grammar demonstrations.</unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Preterite or Imperfect? Classify the Sentence">Generate 8 classification exercises. Each exercise presents a short Spanish sentence with a blank where a verb should go, plus a clear context clue visible in the sentence (a time marker, a trigger word, or a description). The student classifies which tense the blank requires. The options are the same for every exercise: a) Preterite  b) Imperfect.

Sentences should include a variety of clues — preterite time markers (ayer, de repente, una vez, el viernes pasado), imperfect time markers (siempre, todos los días, cuando era niño, normalmente), the interrupted action pattern, and description contexts. Do not include the actual verb form in the blank — just ask which tense is needed. For example:
"De niño, _____ al parque con su padre todos los sábados." → Imperfect
"De repente, ella _____ a correr sin decir nada." → Preterite
"Ayer _____ con mi jefe durante una hora." → Preterite
"Cuando éramos jóvenes, _____ en un pueblo pequeño." → Imperfect</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Choose the Right Tense">Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence with one verb blanked. A context clue (time marker, trigger word, or description) is always present in the sentence and determines which tense is needed. No hints — the context clue is the only guide. Options per blank: the correct preterite form of the verb, the correct imperfect form, and one additional distractor (a wrong-person form of one of those tenses). For example:

1. "Cuando era niña, [jugaba] al fútbol todos los sábados." No hint. Distractors: jugué, jugó.
2. "El lunes pasado, ella [llamó] a su madre por teléfono." No hint. Distractors: llamaba, llama.
3. "Siempre [desayunábamos] juntos antes de ir al colegio." No hint. Distractors: desayunamos, desayunan.
4. "De repente, [apareció] un hombre en la puerta." No hint. Distractors: aparecía, aparece.
5. "Leía un libro cuando de repente [llegó] mi hermano." No hint. Distractors: llegaba, llega.
6. "Normalmente, yo [salía] del trabajo a las seis de la tarde." No hint. Distractors: salí, sale.</unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Tense — Preterite or Imperfect?">Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the verb blanked. The student selects which verb form they heard from 3 options. All options are forms of the same verb — the preterite form, the imperfect form, and one additional distractor (a present tense form or wrong-person form). The student must discriminate the tense from the audio alone — differences in stress, vowel quality, and ending. Use verbs where the two tense forms are auditorily close but distinguishable:

Audio: "Ella habló con su profesora después de clase." Text: "Ella [______] con su profesora después de clase." Options: habló, hablaba, habla.
Audio: "Cuando era joven, vivía en el campo." Text: "Cuando era joven, [______] en el campo." Options: vivió, vivía, vive.
Audio: "De repente, el niño se cayó de la bicicleta." Text: "De repente, el niño [______] de la bicicleta." Options: cayó, caía, cae.
Audio: "Todos los veranos, íbamos a la playa en familia." Text: "Todos los veranos, [______] a la playa en familia." Options: fuimos, íbamos, vamos.
Audio: "Ayer comí en un restaurante nuevo." Text: "Ayer [______] en un restaurante nuevo." Options: comía, comí, como.</unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Verbs That Change Meaning by Tense">

    <unit type="explanation" name="When the Tense Changes the Meaning">Explain that five high-frequency Spanish verbs don't just change aspect when you switch tenses — they carry completely different English meanings. Frame this explicitly as a separate phenomenon from general aspect rules. For each verb, give the preterite meaning, the imperfect meaning, and two natural example sentences (one in each tense). The verbs to cover:

- saber: supe = found out / learned (a new piece of information in a specific moment) vs. sabía = knew (already had that knowledge)
- conocer: conocí = met for the first time vs. conocía = already knew / was acquainted with
- querer: quise = tried to (with some effort, possibly unsuccessfully) vs. quería = wanted (a general desire)
- poder: pude = managed to / succeeded in doing vs. podía = was able to / could (general capability)
- tener: tuve = got / received / experienced (something that came into possession) vs. tenía = had (already possessed)

Finish with a brief note: when translating from English, the English phrase itself reveals which tense to use — "I found out" → supe, "I used to know" → sabía, "she met him" → le conoció, etc.</unit>

    <unit type="word_match" name="Match the Meaning">Generate a matching exercise. The student matches each Spanish verb form to its correct English meaning. Instruction: "Match each Spanish verb form to its correct English meaning." Left column "Spanish Verb" (5 items): supe, sabía, conocí, quise, pude. Right column "English Meaning" (8 items, correct matches plus distractors): I found out, I knew, I met, I tried to, I managed to, I was able to, I wanted to, I already knew.</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Which Meaning? Which Tense?">Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence that uses one of the five meaning-changing verbs (saber, conocer, querer, poder, tener). The context of the sentence makes the intended meaning clear — the student must work out which meaning is required, then select the correct tense form accordingly. Options per blank: the correct preterite form, the correct imperfect form, and one distractor. No hints. For example:

1. "Cuando llegué a la oficina, [supe] que la reunión había sido cancelada." (discovery in a moment → preterite supe) Distractors: sabía, sabe.
2. "De niño, [conocía] bien ese barrio porque vivía allí." (existing familiarity → imperfect conocía) Distractors: conocí, conoce.
3. "Ella [quiso] abrir la ventana pero estaba atascada." (attempted a specific action → preterite quiso) Distractors: quería, quiere.
4. "Nosotros [podíamos] hablar inglés desde pequeños." (general capability in the past → imperfect podíamos) Distractors: pudimos, podemos.
5. "Ayer por fin [tuve] noticias de mi hermano después de semanas sin contacto." (received/got a specific thing → preterite tuve) Distractors: tenía, tengo.
6. "Ella siempre [quería] visitar Japón, pero nunca tuvo la oportunidad." (ongoing desire → imperfect quería) Distractors: quiso, quiere.</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Meaning First, Then Tense">Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence to translate into Spanish. Each sentence uses one of the five meaning-changing verbs, and the English phrasing makes the required Spanish tense unambiguous. The student must identify what the English means, then choose the correct tense form. For example:

1. "I found out about the accident this morning." (→ Supe del accidente esta mañana.)
2. "She always knew the answer before anyone else." (→ Siempre sabía la respuesta antes que nadie.)
3. "We met the new neighbours at the party last Saturday." (→ Conocimos a los nuevos vecinos en la fiesta el sábado pasado.)
4. "He tried to fix the computer but couldn't." (→ Quiso arreglar el ordenador pero no pudo.)
5. "When I was a child, we had a big garden." (→ Cuando era niño/a, teníamos un jardín grande.)</unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Applying the Distinction in Full Context">

    <unit type="explanation" name="A Strategy for Mixed Tense Passages">Keep this short — 1 paragraph plus one worked example. Explain that in mixed-tense passages, the student should approach each verb individually by asking two questions in order: (1) Are there any context clues — a time marker, trigger word, or description word — near this verb? (2) Is the action completed and bounded, or is it describing how things were / what was in progress? Model this process with a 3-sentence passage, showing the verb-by-verb decision for each blank. The worked example should include at least one interrupted action pattern and one meaning-changing verb.</unit>

    <unit type="correction" name="Fix the Tense Errors">Generate 6 correction exercises as a cohesive passage — a short account of a trip to a market. Each sentence has exactly one highlighted tense error: either preterite used where imperfect belongs, or imperfect used where preterite belongs. The errors are already marked — the student only needs to rewrite each highlighted verb with the correct tense form. Mix error types: context-clue violations (using imperfect despite "de repente"), interrupted action errors (using preterite for the background), description errors (using preterite for a state or condition), and at least one meaning-changing verb error. For example:

"Era un sábado tranquilo y [fui] al mercado del barrio." (background state of the day → should be imperfect: iba)
"De repente, [veía] un puesto lleno de especias de colores." (sudden/specific moment → should be preterite: vi)
"[Conocí] bien al vendedor porque siempre compraba allí." (existing familiarity → should be imperfect: conocía)</unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Complete the Story — Choose Your Tense">Generate 6 sentences as a cohesive story about a memorable afternoon. Each sentence has one verb blanked. The hint for each blank is the infinitive form only — the student must decide both the correct tense and the correct conjugation. Context within the surrounding story determines which tense is needed. Include a mix of regular and irregular verbs, and at least one meaning-changing verb. For example:

1. "Era una tarde de verano y el sol [_____] con fuerza." Hint: "brillar". (description → imperfect: brillaba)
2. "De repente, mi amigo Luis [_____] corriendo hacia mí." Hint: "venir". (sudden interruption → preterite: vino)
3. "Me dijo que [_____] una noticia importante." Hint: "tener". (got / received news → preterite: tenía or context of general possession... adjust so context clearly points to imperfect "had something to tell" → tenía)
4. "Nos [_____] en un banco del parque y empezamos a hablar." Hint: "sentar". (specific completed action → preterite: sentamos)
5. "Cuando éramos niños, siempre [_____] a ese parque juntos." Hint: "volver". (habitual past → imperfect: volvíamos)
6. "Esa tarde, [_____] que Luis se marchaba a vivir a otro país." Hint: "saber". (found out — specific discovery → preterite: supe)</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Full-Context Translation">Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence to translate into Spanish. Sentences are more complex than previous translation exercises — they include subordinate clauses, connector words, and require the student to commit to both the correct verb and the correct tense for each verb in the sentence. Each sentence forces at least one clear preterite vs. imperfect decision. For example:

1. "I was reading when the phone rang." (→ Leía cuando sonó el teléfono.)
2. "Every summer, we travelled to the coast with my grandparents." (→ Todos los veranos, viajábamos a la costa con mis abuelos.)
3. "She tried to call him three times but he didn't answer." (→ Quiso llamarle tres veces pero no contestó.)
4. "The children were playing in the garden when it suddenly started to rain." (→ Los niños jugaban en el jardín cuando de repente empezó a llover.)
5. "Yesterday I found out that my colleague had resigned." (→ Ayer supe que mi compañero había dimitido.)</unit>

  </section>

  <review>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Classify: Preterite or Imperfect?">Generate 8 classification exercises mixing all the contexts covered in this lesson: time-marker-based decisions, interrupted action patterns, description contexts, and at least two exercises involving meaning-changing verbs where the intended meaning determines the tense. Each exercise presents a short Spanish sentence (or a sentence with a blank and a bracketed English gloss for meaning-changing verb exercises) and asks the student to classify. Options are the same throughout: a) Preterite  b) Imperfect. For example:

"Normalmente, _____ a casa antes de las siete." → Imperfect
"El miércoles pasado, ella _____ una carta muy importante." (received/got) → Preterite
"Cuando éramos jóvenes, _____ mucho tiempo al aire libre." → Imperfect
"De repente, todos _____ a reír al mismo tiempo." → Preterite
"Él _____ a Juan en una conferencia hace dos años." (met for the first time) → Preterite</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Mixed Tense Story">Generate 8 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story — someone recounting a memorable day from their past. Each sentence has one verb blanked. No hints. Options per blank: the correct preterite form, the correct imperfect form, and one additional distractor. The story should include a variety of contexts: habitual background, interrupted action, specific completed events, and at least two meaning-changing verbs (e.g. saber and conocer) where the story context determines the meaning and therefore the tense. The story should flow naturally when completed correctly.</unit>

    <unit type="dialogue_write" name="Talking About the Past">Generate a dialogue completion exercise. The user is talking to a Spanish friend, Sofía, who is asking about a significant moment from the user's past. The dialogue is 8 turns (4 each). The user's lines have verbs blanked. Hint for each blank: the infinitive form only. No options — the student must type the correct conjugated form in the correct tense. Sofía's questions establish the time frame and context for each of the user's responses, naturally eliciting a mix of preterite (for specific events and discoveries) and imperfect (for descriptions, habits, and ongoing states). Include at least one meaning-changing verb in the user's lines. For example:

Sofía: ¿Cuándo decidiste aprender a tocar la guitarra?
Tú: [_____] la guitarra de mi abuelo cuando [_____] unos doce años. Hints: "encontrar", "tener".
Sofía: ¿Y cuándo empezaste las clases?
Tú: [_____] con un profesor ese mismo verano. Hint: "empezar".
Sofía: ¿Te resultó difícil al principio?
Tú: Sí, al principio no [_____] tocar bien, pero con práctica lo [_____]. Hints: "poder", "conseguir".</unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Hear and Transcribe — Past Tense Mix">Generate 5 dictation exercises. Each sentence uses both preterite and imperfect naturally within a single sentence — the student must hear and transcribe the correct tense forms for each verb. Sentences are 7–9 words. No context lines. Mix regular and irregular verbs, including at least one meaning-changing verb. Focus on sentences where the audio distinction between the preterite and imperfect forms is the key difficulty (stress patterns, vowel endings). For example:

"Leía el periódico cuando sonó el teléfono."
"De niño siempre quería un perro, pero nunca tuve uno."
"Supe la verdad cuando llegué a casa esa noche."
"El restaurante estaba lleno, así que comimos en la terraza."
"Ella conocía la ciudad porque vivía allí de pequeña."</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Final Translation Challenge">Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence to translate into Spanish. These are the most demanding translation exercises in the lesson — each sentence requires at least two tense decisions, and at least two sentences include meaning-changing verbs. No scaffolding of any kind. For example:

1. "When I was young, I knew all my neighbours, but then we moved." (→ Cuando era joven, conocía a todos mis vecinos, pero luego nos mudamos.)
2. "She tried to find a taxi but there weren't any available." (→ Quiso encontrar un taxi pero no había ninguno disponible.)
3. "It was a cold morning and the streets were empty when he suddenly saw her." (→ Era una mañana fría y las calles estaban vacías cuando de repente la vio.)
4. "We always went to the mountains in winter and stayed in a small cabin." (→ Siempre íbamos a la montaña en invierno y nos quedábamos en una cabaña pequeña.)
5. "I found out yesterday that she had already known about the plan for weeks." (→ Ayer supe que ella ya sabía el plan desde hacía semanas.)</unit>

  </review>

</lesson>

output:
<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to do

This is Lesson 4 of Week 3 — a synthesis/application lesson, not a grammar introduction lesson. The student already knows:
- How to conjugate the imperfect (Lesson 1)
- When to use the imperfect (habitual, ongoing, descriptive) and how it contrasts with the preterite at the sentence level (Lessons 2 & 3)

What hasn't been taught yet is **narrative layering** — how the two tenses work *simultaneously in a connected story* rather than in isolated sentence pairs. The distinction from Lesson 3 was "which tense fits THIS sentence?" The skill here is "how do both tenses live inside the same story, doing different structural jobs?"

The learning outcomes (stripping the activity suggestions):
1. Understand how preterite and imperfect divide narrative labour — imperfect sets the scene/background, preterite advances the plot
2. Recognize and choose between the two tenses in connected narrative passages
3. Produce a short, layered narrative using both tenses appropriately

Lesson 5 (next) is the full review — 300-word personal narrative. So this lesson must bring the student to the point of being able to write 150-200 words of coherent layered narrative confidently.

## Step 2: Breaking into sections

**Section 1 — Narrative Layering in Action**
The concept of *narrative layers* is new even though the grammar isn't. This section introduces the framework (imperfect = backdrop / preterite = plot), then builds recognition and guided production in a connected story context.

Section goal: *The student can read a narrative passage and correctly identify/choose whether each verb should be preterite or imperfect, understanding its narrative role.*

**Section 2 — Building and Writing Your Own Narrative**
The student moves from recognizing tense choices in someone else's story to constructing their own. This section ramps through guided production (word order, typed blanks, fixing errors) to full free production (translation, then open writing).

Section goal: *The student can produce a 150-200 word personal narrative that naturally layers preterite and imperfect, with scene-setting background and specific sequenced events.*

These are genuinely different cognitive tasks — recognition/guided practice vs. personal production — so two sections is correct. PARALLEL CHECK: Section 1 and Section 2 are NOT parallel in structure because Section 1 is recognition-focused (input → guided choice) and Section 2 is production-focused (output → free writing). Different structure is justified by content.

## Step 3: Units per section

**Section 1: Narrative Layering in Action**

1. **explanation** — Introduce the narrative layering framework: imperfect as the "camera panning across the scene" (what the world was like, what people were doing habitually, how things felt) and preterite as "specific events that happened and moved the story forward." Concrete example narrative with colour-coded tenses. Explain signal words/triggers.

2. **conversation** — Two characters sharing a childhood memory in Spanish. The dialogue naturally uses both tenses: imperfect for describing how things used to be / what was happening, preterite for the specific events that occurred. Shows narrative rhythm in spoken Spanish before the student has to handle it in exercises.

3. **mcq** — 6 sentences pulled from a narrative context. Student classifies each as using the preterite or imperfect — and importantly, the brief surrounding context is given so they understand WHY. Same options throughout (Preterite / Imperfect). This tests recognition of the narrative role.

4. **fill_in_blanks** — A cohesive story of 8 sentences (a person describing a memorable childhood day). Each sentence has one verb blanked. Options include the correct preterite or imperfect form of the verb plus 1-2 distractors. The student must read narrative context to decide tense, then pick the right form.

5. **listening_fill** — 5 sentences from narrative passages. Audio plays the complete sentence; student identifies whether they heard the preterite or imperfect form. Options are same-verb forms in different tenses (e.g. corrió / corría / corre). This is valuable here because preterite/imperfect distinctions are often subtle aurally (stress patterns, ending sounds) and the student benefits from training their ear on narrative sentences specifically.

**Section 2: Building and Writing Your Own Narrative**

1. **explanation** — Brief scaffolding on structuring a personal story: how to open with imperfect scene-setting, then introduce preterite events, and how the two tenses can interweave. Provide a short structural template with a worked example. This is short — the grammar is known, but the construction strategy is new.

2. **word_order** — 5 scrambled sentences mixing both tenses in narrative contexts. Forces the student to reconstruct correctly tensed sentences they couldn't have simply copied, building fluency with how these tenses sit inside sentence structures.

3. **write_in_blanks** — Cohesive story of 7 sentences (a different childhood narrative — a visit to a grandparent's house, for example). Verb blanked in each sentence. No options — the student must type the correct form based on context. Hints provide the infinitive but NOT the tense, so the student must decide both tense and form themselves. This is genuine production within a supported context.

4. **correction** — A short narrative passage (6-7 sentences) with 6 highlighted tense errors — places where the wrong tense was chosen. The student corrects each. This is different from the fill_in_blanks above: here the student is diagnosing the *narrative role* error (e.g. a completed past event written in imperfect, or a background description written in preterite) rather than filling a gap.

5. **translation** — 5 English sentences from a childhood narrative → Spanish. These are complex enough to require choosing the right tense AND the right conjugated form. The English phrasing helps the student infer tense (e.g. "I was playing when my dad arrived" makes both tenses explicit in English). This is pre-writing scaffolding for the writing task.

6. **writing_practice** — Write a 150-200 word story about a memorable childhood moment. Objectives: use at least 5 preterite verbs to advance the narrative, use at least 4 imperfect verbs for scene-setting or ongoing description, and use at least one time expression that signals each tense (e.g. *de repente*, *siempre*, *mientras*).

## Step 4: Review section

The review is integration. Both sections have pushed the student to production. The review needs a fresh context with no scaffolding, testing the whole skill: read a connected narrative context, produce the correct form, and handle both tenses interchangeably.

1. **write_in_blanks** — A fresh cohesive narrative story (8-9 sentences, a person describing a school trip or family holiday from childhood). All verbs blanked, infinitive as hint but no tense indication — the student must determine everything from narrative context. No options.

2. **translation** — 5 English → Spanish narrative sentences, more complex than in Section 2. Include one sentence with "was doing X when Y happened" (imperfect + preterite interruption pattern) as this is the canonical narrative test case.

3. **dictation** — 4 sentences from a childhood narrative, each 8-10 words. Mix of preterite and imperfect. The student must transcribe correctly including accents. This tests whether the student can hear AND spell the distinction — a meaningful final challenge that rewards the full week's grammar work.

## Step 5: Supplementary units

- **listening_fill** added to Section 1 (after fill_in_blanks): Valuable here because preterite/imperfect auditory discrimination on narrative-length sentences is a genuine challenge students face. The stress difference between *corrió* and *corría* is easy to miss.
- **dictation** in the review: The student has reached full production by this point. Dictation tests the auditory-to-written pipeline and reinforces accent marks on these tense endings — a known student error.
- No **listening_word_order** — the word_order unit in Section 2 already handles sentence structure practice, and adding the listening version would be redundant without adding significant new value. The dictation serves the listening production goal more meaningfully at this stage.

**Why no dialogue units?** The exercises throughout this lesson are narrative-based (third person stories, personal anecdotes) rather than conversational. The grammar is being applied in written storytelling, not dialogue. The conversation unit in Section 1 provides the spoken model. Adding dialogue_fill or dialogue_write would misrepresent the genre — people don't narrate layered past stories in rapid back-and-forth dialogue in a way that exercises both tenses cleanly. The fill_in_blanks and write_in_blanks in cohesive story format serve the same scaffolding role and are genre-appropriate.

</plan>

<lesson>

  <context>In this lesson, you'll learn how to use the preterite and imperfect tenses together to tell stories in Spanish. Rather than choosing between them in isolated sentences, you'll practise layering them — using the imperfect to paint the background and the preterite to move the story forward — and finish by writing your own short narrative about a childhood memory.</context>

  <section name="Narrative Layering in Action">

    <unit type="explanation" name="How Stories Use Both Tenses">
      Explain the concept of narrative layering in Spanish storytelling. The core idea: the imperfect is the "backdrop" — it describes what the world was like, what was happening continuously or habitually, how people felt, and what things looked like. The preterite is the "plot engine" — it describes specific, completed events that happened and moved the story forward. Use a short 4-5 sentence example narrative in Spanish (about a childhood afternoon at a park, for instance) and clearly annotate which verbs are imperfect (and why) vs preterite (and why). Then list common narrative cues: imperfect tends to appear with words like *siempre*, *todos los días*, *mientras*, *de niño/a*, *era*, and in descriptive opening sentences; preterite tends to appear with *de repente*, *entonces*, *un día*, *por fin*, *después*, and any verb that "changes the scene." Keep the tone practical and story-focused — the student knows both tenses, this is about how they cooperate inside a narrative.
    </unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="A Childhood Memory">
      Generate a single conversation of 10-12 lines between two friends, Elena (early 30s, female) and Marcos (early 30s, male). They are chatting over coffee about a vivid childhood memory. Elena describes a day from her childhood — what her neighbourhood was like, what she and her friends used to do, and then a specific event that happened one afternoon that she still remembers clearly. Marcos asks a couple of follow-up questions and briefly shares his own related memory. The dialogue should naturally use imperfect verbs for background descriptions and habitual actions (what the street was like, what they always did, how Elena felt) and preterite verbs for the specific events that occurred that day. The conversation should feel warm and natural — two adults reminiscing — not like a grammar exercise. Dialogue only; no annotations or translations.
    </unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="What Narrative Role Is This Verb Playing?">
      Test whether the student can identify the narrative role of a verb in context. Explanation: "For each sentence, decide whether the highlighted verb is being used as preterite (a completed event) or imperfect (background/description/habit)." Generate 6 exercises. Each exercise presents one sentence from a narrative, with a brief one-line context (in English) indicating where in the story this sentence appears — e.g. "This is the opening line of a story about a summer holiday" or "This sentence follows 'Then, suddenly...'" The Spanish sentence contains one bold/highlighted verb form. The student classifies it. Options are the same for every exercise: a) Preterite (completed event) b) Imperfect (background / description / habit). Use a mix of clear and slightly tricky cases — for instance, a clearly background imperfect description, a preterite interrupting event, a habitual imperfect, a preterite sequence event, and one where the imperfect is describing someone's emotional state during a scene.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="A Day at the Lake — Choose the Tense">
      Generate 8 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story. The story is a first-person narrative about a memorable childhood day at a lake — it should have a clear structure: imperfect scene-setting at the start (describing the lake, the weather, what the family usually did there), then preterite events (something specific that happened that day — perhaps the narrator learned to swim, or something unexpected occurred), and a brief imperfect-flavoured closing reflection. Each sentence has exactly one verb blanked. No hints — the narrative context itself should guide the tense choice. For each blank, provide 3 options: the correct form (either preterite or imperfect) plus 2 distractors that are forms of the same verb in the other tense or a different person. For example:
      1. "El lago [______] a las afueras de nuestro pueblo." Options: estaba, estuvo, estábamos. (imperfect — description)
      2. "Un verano, mi padre [______] que íbamos a pasar el fin de semana allí." Options: decidió, decidía, decidieron. (preterite — specific event)
      Make all 8 sentences feel like a real, connected story. Do not use the same verb twice.
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Tense — Preterite vs Imperfect">
      Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is from a narrative context (someone describing a past experience or story). The audio plays the complete sentence including the target verb. The text displays the sentence with the verb blanked. The student selects which verb form they heard. All options for each exercise must be forms of the SAME verb in different tenses, so the student must discriminate the tense and person from audio alone — this tests the sonic difference between preterite and imperfect endings. Use 3 options per blank. Target these contrasts: -ar imperfect (-aba) vs -ar preterite (-ó/-aron), -er/-ir imperfect (-ía) vs preterite (-ió/-ieron), and first person singular imperfect (-aba/-ía) vs third person preterite. For example:
      Audio: "De repente, el perro [salió] corriendo hacia el bosque." Options: salió, salía, sale.
      Audio: "Cuando era pequeño, mi abuela siempre [cocinaba] paella los domingos." Options: cocinó, cocinaba, cocina.
      Audio: "Esa noche, mis padres [llegaron] tarde a casa." Options: llegaban, llegaron, llegan.
      Sentences should be 7-11 words, drawn from varied narrative scenarios (childhood, a trip, a school day, a family event).
    </unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Building and Writing Your Own Narrative">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Structuring Your Own Story">
      Give a short, practical explanation of how to structure a personal story using both tenses. Cover three moves: (1) Opening with imperfect — set the scene with 2-3 imperfect sentences describing the time, place, and atmosphere; (2) Introducing the preterite — signal a specific event with a trigger phrase (*un día*, *de repente*, *entonces*) and use preterite to narrate what happened, step by step; (3) Weaving them together — show how imperfect and preterite can appear in the same sentence, e.g. *Estaba jugando en el jardín cuando escuché un ruido extraño* (imperfect for what was ongoing, preterite for the interruption). Provide one fully worked example of this three-part structure using a childhood memory of about 5-6 sentences, with each sentence's tense choice briefly annotated. Keep it concise — this is a strategic scaffolding note, not a grammar re-explanation.
    </unit>

    <unit type="word_order" name="Assemble the Narrative Sentences">
      Generate 5 word order exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence from a narrative context mixing preterite and imperfect. Sentences should be 8-11 words. Include sentences that: contain both an imperfect and preterite verb in the same sentence (the interruption pattern), place time expressions in different positions, and use first and third person. Provide an English translation for each sentence so the student knows what they're building. For example:
      1. "Mientras dormía, alguien llamó a la puerta de repente." — "While he was sleeping, someone suddenly knocked on the door."
      2. "Éramos muy jóvenes cuando visitamos esa ciudad por primera vez." — "We were very young when we visited that city for the first time."
      3. "El cielo estaba despejado y hacía calor cuando salimos de casa." — "The sky was clear and it was warm when we left the house."
    </unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="A Visit to Grandma's House">
      Generate 7 write-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story. The story is a first-person childhood narrative about visiting a grandparent's house — it should open with imperfect scene-setting (what the house was like, what Grandma always did, how the narrator felt), then move into specific preterite events (something that happened on one particular visit), and close with a brief preterite statement wrapping up the day. Each sentence has exactly one verb blanked. Since there are no options, include the infinitive as a hint for every blank — the student must decide both the correct tense AND the correct conjugated form themselves. Hints should give only the infinitive (e.g. "llegar"), not the tense. For example:
      1. "La casa de mi abuela siempre [______] a pan recién horneado." Hint: "oler". (imperfect — description)
      2. "Un sábado de diciembre, mi madre me [______] temprano." Hint: "despertar". (preterite — specific event)
      3. "Mientras [______] en el jardín, escuché que alguien tocaba el piano." Hint: "jugar". (imperfect — ongoing action)
      Do not repeat any verb. Make the story feel genuinely connected, not a sequence of unrelated sentences.
    </unit>

    <unit type="correction" name="Fix the Tense Errors in This Story">
      Generate 6 correction exercises as a cohesive narrative passage — a short story about a school trip a child took. Each sentence has exactly one highlighted error: the wrong tense was used (either imperfect where preterite is needed, or preterite where imperfect is needed). The student must replace the highlighted incorrect form with the correct tense form. Distribute error types evenly: 3 sentences where preterite was used but imperfect is needed (e.g. a description or background state written in preterite), and 3 sentences where imperfect was used but preterite is needed (e.g. a completed specific event written in imperfect). Make sure the incorrect forms are real, plausible Spanish verb forms — the kind of error a genuine learner would make. For example:
      "El autocar [salió: salía] a las ocho todos los días de aquella semana." (preterite used for habitual action — should be imperfect)
      "De repente, la profesora [anunciaba: anunció] que habíamos llegado." (imperfect used for sudden event — should be preterite)
      Ensure the passage reads as a coherent story once all errors are corrected.
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Childhood Story Sentences — English to Spanish">
      Generate 5 translation exercises, each a single English sentence to translate into Spanish. The sentences form a loose mini-narrative about a childhood memory (a birthday party, for instance). They must require the student to choose between preterite and imperfect and produce the correct conjugated form. Include: one imperfect descriptive sentence, one preterite sequence event, one sentence with the classic interruption pattern (*was doing X when Y happened* — requiring both tenses in one sentence), one imperfect habitual sentence, and one preterite sentence marking the end of the event. For example:
      1. "The garden was full of children and everyone was laughing." (imperfect — scene description)
      2. "Suddenly, my mum brought out a huge chocolate cake." (preterite — specific event)
      3. "We were playing outside when it started to rain." (imperfect + preterite interruption)
      4. "Every year, my grandmother made the same cake for me." (imperfect — habitual)
      5. "We went home when the party finished." (preterite — end of event)
    </unit>

    <unit type="writing_practice" name="A Memorable Childhood Moment">
      Generate a writing prompt asking the student to write a short story in Spanish (150-200 words) about a memorable moment from their childhood — it could be a holiday, a family celebration, a school day, or any moment that has stayed with them. Objectives:
      1. Use at least 5 preterite verbs to narrate the specific events that happened (what occurred, in sequence).
      2. Use at least 4 imperfect verbs for scene-setting, background description, or ongoing actions that framed the events (what the place was like, what people were doing, how you felt).
      3. Include at least one sentence that uses both tenses together — an ongoing imperfect action interrupted by a preterite event (e.g. *Estaba... cuando...*).
    </unit>

  </section>

  <review>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="A Summer Evening — Complete the Story">
      Generate 9 write-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive narrative story. The story is a third-person narrative about a child spending an evening at a village festival with his family — it should have clear narrative structure: imperfect opening (what the festival was always like, the atmosphere, what people wore), preterite events (what happened that particular night), and a brief imperfect/preterite close. Each sentence has exactly one verb blanked. Provide only the infinitive as a hint for every blank — no tense indication. The student must determine tense AND form from context alone. Do not repeat any verb. Vary the subjects (yo, él, ellos, nosotros, etc.) and include at least one sentence with both an imperfect and a preterite verb, where only the preterite verb is blanked. For example:
      1. "El festival [______] lugar cada agosto en la plaza mayor del pueblo." Hint: "tener". (imperfect — habitual event)
      2. "Esa noche, Tomás y su padre [______] tarde porque el coche no arrancaba." Hint: "llegar". (preterite — specific event)
      3. "Mientras los músicos [tocaban], una tormenta [______] de repente." — only the preterite verb is blanked: Hint: "comenzar". (both tenses present, only preterite blanked)
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Narrative Sentences — English to Spanish">
      Generate 5 translation exercises, each a single English sentence to translate into Spanish. These should be more complex than earlier translation exercises — include multi-clause sentences, connectors, and tense-switching within one sentence. At least two sentences must require both preterite and imperfect in the same sentence. Vary the persons and verbs. Do not reuse any sentence structure from Section 2's translation unit. For example:
      1. "The streets were narrow and the lights were on when we arrived at the village." (imperfect descriptions + preterite arrival)
      2. "He was reading in his room when his sister called him for dinner." (imperfect + preterite interruption)
      3. "As a child, I was afraid of the dark, but that night I felt brave." (imperfect habit/state + preterite specific feeling)
      4. "She opened the old box and found a photograph she had never seen before." (preterite sequence)
      5. "The music was playing and everyone was dancing when the lights suddenly went out." (two imperfects + preterite interruption)
    </unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Narrative Sentences — Listen and Write">
      Generate 4 dictation exercises. Each is a sentence from a narrative context using the preterite and/or imperfect tense. The student listens and types the full sentence exactly — including correct spelling, accent marks, and punctuation. Sentences should be 7-10 words. Include: one imperfect description sentence, one preterite event sentence, one sentence combining both tenses (the interruption pattern), and one preterite sequence sentence. Provide a brief English context line for the first sentence only to orient the listener. Make sure each sentence contains at least one accent-marked verb form (e.g. *llegó*, *estábamos*, *salió*, *había*) so that orthographic accuracy is genuinely tested. For example:
      "Aquella tarde, el sol brillaba y hacía calor." Context: "The narrator describing a summer afternoon."
      "De repente, escuché un ruido detrás de la puerta."
      "Mientras comíamos, mi abuelo empezó a contarnos una historia."
      "Salimos del cine y caminamos hasta la plaza."
    </unit>

  </review>

</lesson>

Month goal: This month focuses on solidifying your command of the present, preterite, and imperfect tenses with an emphasis on accuracy and natural usage. You'll practice common mistakes and build confidence in everyday conversations through targeted exercises that highlight when to use each tense correctly.

Previous week (Week 1): Present Tense Precision and Common Errors
  Goal: Establish a strong foundation by identifying and correcting frequent present tense mistakes. This week covers regular and irregular verbs in present tense, focusing on conjugation accuracy and real-world application for daily interactions.
  Lessons:
    - Present Tense Regular Verbs: Building Accuracy: Reviewing regular present tense verb conjugations in Spanish (-ar, -er, -ir) and avoiding common errors.
    - Present Tense Irregular Verbs: Practical Focus: - Cover essential irregular verbs in present tense (ser, estar, ir, tener, hacer, poder, querer, saber)
- Distinguish between similar irregular verbs (ser vs. estar) through comparative exercises
- Fill-in-the-blank activities with irregular verbs in conversational contexts
- Translate 10 English sentences into Spanish, focusing on selecting correct irregular verb forms
    - Reflexive Verbs in Present Tense: - Understand reflexive verb structure and when reflexive forms are necessary (levantarse, llamarse, sentirse)
- Practice reflexive pronoun placement and conjugation patterns
- Complete sentences with correct reflexive pronoun and verb conjugation
- Write a daily morning routine using 6-8 reflexive verbs in present tense
    - Present Indicative in Conversational Scenarios: - Apply present tense to realistic social situations (introductions, ordering food, asking directions)
- Practice with dialogue-based fill-in-the-blank exercises
- Distinguish between formal and informal present tense usage (tú vs. usted)
- Create short conversational exchanges using present tense verbs in context
    - Present Tense Accuracy Review: Verbs, Reflexives, and Context: - Review all present tense concepts: regular, irregular, and reflexive verbs
- Complete mixed exercises combining all verb types in various conversational contexts
- Identify and correct 15 common present tense errors in provided sentences
- Production: Write a 200-word text about yourself, your job, and your daily habits using accurate present tense

Previous week (Week 2): Preterite Tense Mastery and Regular Patterns
  Goal: Build confidence with the preterite tense by mastering regular patterns, understanding conjugation rules, and practicing common irregular forms. This week emphasizes the distinction between actions completed in the past and prepares you for combining tenses naturally.
  Lessons:
    - Preterite Regular Verbs: Foundations: - Learn regular preterite conjugations for -ar, -er, -ir verbs with clear pattern recognition
- Understand preterite endings and identify regular patterns (habé, comí, viví)
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises with high-frequency regular verbs in preterite
- Translate 10 English past tense sentences into Spanish using regular preterite verbs
    - Preterite Irregular Verbs: High-Frequency Forms: - Cover the most common irregular preterite verbs (ser/fue, ir/fue, tener/tuve, estar/estuve, hacer/hice, poder/pude, poner/puse)
- Understand irregular stem patterns and how to recognize them
- Practice conjugation through flashcard-style activities with context sentences
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises using high-frequency irregular preterites in realistic narratives
    - Preterite vs. Present Perfect: When to Use Each: - Distinguish between preterite (simple past) and present perfect (have done) in Spanish usage
- Understand regional differences (Spain vs. Latin America) in usage preferences
- Complete sentences choosing between preterite and present perfect forms
- Rewrite 8 sentences, switching between preterite and present perfect where appropriate
    - Preterite in Narrative and Personal Stories: - Apply preterite tense to storytelling and personal anecdotes (vacation, weekend, past event)
- Practice with narrative passages that use preterite for completed actions
- Fill-in-the-blank in short story excerpts using appropriate preterite forms
- Write a 150-word paragraph about something you did last weekend using accurate preterite tense
    - Preterite Mastery Review: Regular, Irregular, and Context: - Review all preterite concepts: regular patterns, common irregulars, and narrative usage
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises with preterite verbs in varied contexts
- Identify correct vs. incorrect preterite conjugations in 12-15 sentences
- Production: Write 300 words describing a past event, trip, or significant day using varied preterite verbs

  Goal: Master the imperfect tense to describe ongoing, habitual, or background actions in the past. This week teaches you when to use imperfect versus preterite, allowing you to tell more complex, nuanced stories about your past experiences.

- Distinguish between momentary actions (preterite) and continuous/habitual actions (imperfect)

    - Past Tenses Mastery Review: Preterite, Imperfect, and Narrative Cohesion: - Review preterite vs. imperfect distinctions, conjugations, and usage contexts

output:
<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to do

This is the **final lesson of Week 3** and a **mastery review lesson** — no new content is being introduced. The student has spent four lessons learning:
- Imperfect conjugations (regular + irregular)
- When to use imperfect (habitual, ongoing, descriptive)
- Preterite vs. imperfect distinctions
- Layering both tenses in narrative

The lesson description lists activity types (fill-in-the-blank, identify tense choices, write a narrative) — these are noise. The real learning outcomes are:
1. **Accurately produce** preterite and imperfect conjugations (including irregulars) under pressure, without scaffolding
2. **Discriminate** between preterite, imperfect, and (as a stretch) present perfect based on context and meaning
3. **Explain** why a particular tense was chosen in a passage (meta-cognitive)
4. **Produce** an extended personal narrative (300 words) that layers both tenses naturally

The present perfect mention is legitimate — it was covered in Week 2 Lesson 3, so it belongs in the complex "mixing" exercises but is not a teaching target for this lesson.

This is a review lesson, so sections should be **practice-heavy**, not teaching-heavy. Explanations are orientation/recap only. The arc of the lesson leads toward the 300-word narrative production at the end.

---

## Step 2: Breaking into sections

**Section 1: Accuracy Under Pressure — Forms and Discrimination**
By the end of this section, the student can accurately produce and select correct preterite and imperfect forms in varied sentence contexts, and can articulate why a particular tense was chosen.

Reasoning: Even in a review lesson, the student needs a re-grounding pass through forms before narrative complexity. This section focuses on accuracy and tense-choice judgment — the building blocks of the narrative production goal. The MCQ here is for the meta-cognitive "why was this tense used?" task, not for sentence blanks. Correction tests whether they can spot wrong tense choices in connected text. Write-in-blanks and listening units round out production and auditory discrimination.

**Section 2: Narrative Mastery — Layering Both Tenses**
By the end of this section, the student can produce a coherent narrative passage (100–150 words) that naturally combines preterite and imperfect in appropriate roles.

Reasoning: Section 1 established accuracy on individual sentences. Section 2 lifts to narrative — longer passages, combined tenses, and eventually free production. This section includes a model conversation (showing the tenses working together in speech), complex fill-in-blanks on a cohesive story, a dialogue write (producing both tenses in conversational context), translation (tense-discriminating sentences), and a writing prompt as a pre-task before the 300-word finale in the review.

**Review:** Pure integration — mixing preterite, imperfect, and present perfect across correction, complex write-in-blanks, translation, dictation, and the 300-word narrative production.

---

## Step 3: Units per section

### Section 1: Accuracy Under Pressure

- **explanation** — Recap orientation: key irregular forms in both tenses, summary of the preterite/imperfect distinction with trigger phrases and context signals. Not new teaching — a reference frame for the exercises that follow.
- **mcq** — "Why was this tense used?" Each exercise shows a sentence and the student selects the reason from options (e.g. "habitual past action," "completed specific event," "background description," "interrupted action"). 8 exercises. This tests meta-cognitive understanding — understanding the WHY, not just the form. Same 4 options across all exercises.
- **fill_in_blanks** — Isolated sentences, blank is the conjugated verb, student selects correct preterite OR imperfect form from options (no hints — context clues must determine the tense, not an infinitive hint). Distractors are the same verb in the other tense and one other form. 6 exercises.
- **write_in_blanks** — Same type of exercise but typed. Mix of regular and irregular verbs. Cohesive short story format so the student has narrative context. Hint: infinitive form only (student must still determine which tense). 6 exercises.
- **correction** — Isolated sentences with one highlighted error each. Error type: wrong tense choice (e.g. preterite used where imperfect is needed or vice versa). The student must replace the wrong form with the correct tense form. 6 exercises.
- **listening_fill** — Student listens to sentences and selects which verb form they heard. Options are preterite vs. imperfect vs. present forms of the same verb. 5 exercises. Pure auditory discrimination.

### Section 2: Narrative Mastery

- **explanation** — Orientation on narrative layering: imperfect = scene-setting/background, preterite = plot events. Also briefly flags where present perfect might appear in natural speech (for the review). 2–3 sentences of framing — not a grammar lesson, a narrative craft reminder.
- **conversation** — Two characters discussing a past experience (e.g. a travel story or a childhood memory). Natural dialogue that layers imperfect descriptions/habits with preterite specific events. 8–10 turns. Shows the student what natural combined-tense narrative sounds like in speech.
- **fill_in_blanks** — Cohesive story (6–8 sentences). Mix of preterite and imperfect blanks. No hints — context determines the tense. Distractors include both tense options and one irregular form of the same verb. More complex and longer sentences than Section 1.
- **dialogue_write** — User completes a conversation about a past experience (e.g. telling a friend about something that happened). Blanks target conjugated preterite and imperfect verbs. Hint: infinitive only. 8 turns, 5–6 blanks total. Forces production of both tenses in authentic conversational context.
- **translation** — 5 English → Spanish exercises. Each sentence requires a tense judgment (some clearly preterite, some clearly imperfect, one or two ambiguous-seeming but decidable from context). These are slightly complex — compound clauses or "was doing X when Y happened" structures that force both tenses in one sentence.
- **writing_practice** — 100–150 word narrative about a specific memory. Objectives: use at least 4 preterite verbs for events, at least 3 imperfect verbs for background/description, include at least one sentence that uses both tenses together (e.g. "estaba comiendo cuando..."). This is the scaffolded pre-task before the 300-word review finale.
- **dictation** — 4 narrative sentences (cohesive mini-passage) using both preterite and imperfect. Tests whether the student can hear, segment, and transcribe both tense forms accurately. No context line — they've been working with narrative for the whole section.

### Parallel structure check:
Both sections start with explanation (orientation), move through recognition exercises, and end at production. Section 1 uses more isolated/classification work; Section 2 uses more connected narrative and extended production. These differ for content reasons: Section 1's job is accuracy and discrimination, Section 2's job is narrative integration. No unwarranted variety.

---

## Step 4: Review section

Pure integration — no explanation. Tests all three tenses together (preterite, imperfect, present perfect) in complex mixed contexts, leading to the 300-word final production.

- **correction** — Cohesive passage (8–9 sentences, a personal narrative) with 1 highlighted error per sentence. Mix of error types: wrong tense choice, wrong conjugation form (especially irregulars), preterite/imperfect swap, one present perfect vs. preterite confusion. This mirrors the "identify tense choices" goal from the lesson description.
- **fill_in_blanks** — Cohesive story mixing all three tenses (preterite, imperfect, present perfect). 6 sentences, 1 blank each. No hints. Distractors include forms from all three tenses so the student must discriminate between all three simultaneously.
- **write_in_blanks** — Longer cohesive narrative passage (8 sentences). Student types conjugated verbs. Mix of preterite and imperfect. Hint: infinitive only. Forces recall without options — highest pressure before free writing.
- **translation** — 5 exercises, English → Spanish. These are the "complex mixed" exercises: some sentences use all three tenses, some are compound, some require "was doing when" structures. Tests full production in translation context.
- **dictation** — 4 sentences from a narrative. Mix of preterite, imperfect, and one present perfect sentence. Tests auditory transcription at the highest difficulty in this lesson — students must hear AND reproduce three different tense forms accurately.
- **writing_practice** — The 300-word personal narrative. Objectives: use at least 6 preterite verbs for completed events, at least 5 imperfect verbs for background/description/habits, include at least 2 sentences using both tenses together (interrupted actions or contrasts), use at least one time marker for each tense (e.g. "de repente" for preterite, "siempre" or "cuando era niño/a" for imperfect).

---

## Step 5: Supplementary listening units

- Section 1: **listening_fill** added ✓ — auditory discrimination between preterite and imperfect forms is a genuine challenge at this stage; students confuse "habló" / "hablaba" in speech.
- Section 2: **dictation** added ✓ — student has reached production level in this section, so dictation is appropriate. Narrative sentences combine both tenses, testing whether they can hear and reproduce the shift.
- Review: **dictation** added ✓ — extends the listening challenge to include present perfect forms alongside the two past tenses.

No `listening_word_order` included — the student is past the introductory listening stage; `listening_fill` and `dictation` are more appropriate difficulty levels for this mastery review.

Lesson Title: Near Future: Ir + A + Infinitive
Description: - Learn the present tense ir + a + infinitive structure for discussing immediate future plans

curriculumContext:
Month 1: Mastering Core Tenses and Accuracy
Month goal: This month focuses on solidifying your command of the present, preterite, and imperfect tenses with an emphasis on accuracy and natural usage. You'll practice common mistakes and build confidence in everyday conversations through targeted exercises that highlight when to use each tense correctly.

Previous week (Week 2): Preterite Tense Mastery and Regular Patterns
  Goal: Build confidence with the preterite tense by mastering regular patterns, understanding conjugation rules, and practicing common irregular forms. This week emphasizes the distinction between actions completed in the past and prepares you for combining tenses naturally.
  Lessons:
    - Preterite Regular Verbs: Foundations: - Learn regular preterite conjugations for -ar, -er, -ir verbs with clear pattern recognition
- Understand preterite endings and identify regular patterns (habé, comí, viví)
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises with high-frequency regular verbs in preterite
- Translate 10 English past tense sentences into Spanish using regular preterite verbs
    - Preterite Irregular Verbs: High-Frequency Forms: - Cover the most common irregular preterite verbs (ser/fue, ir/fue, tener/tuve, estar/estuve, hacer/hice, poder/pude, poner/puse)
- Understand irregular stem patterns and how to recognize them
- Practice conjugation through flashcard-style activities with context sentences
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises using high-frequency irregular preterites in realistic narratives
    - Preterite vs. Present Perfect: When to Use Each: - Distinguish between preterite (simple past) and present perfect (have done) in Spanish usage
- Understand regional differences (Spain vs. Latin America) in usage preferences
- Complete sentences choosing between preterite and present perfect forms
- Rewrite 8 sentences, switching between preterite and present perfect where appropriate
    - Preterite in Narrative and Personal Stories: - Apply preterite tense to storytelling and personal anecdotes (vacation, weekend, past event)
- Practice with narrative passages that use preterite for completed actions
- Fill-in-the-blank in short story excerpts using appropriate preterite forms
- Write a 150-word paragraph about something you did last weekend using accurate preterite tense
    - Preterite Mastery Review: Regular, Irregular, and Context: - Review all preterite concepts: regular patterns, common irregulars, and narrative usage
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises with preterite verbs in varied contexts
- Identify correct vs. incorrect preterite conjugations in 12-15 sentences
- Production: Write 300 words describing a past event, trip, or significant day using varied preterite verbs

Previous week (Week 3): Imperfect Tense and Past Continuous Action
  Goal: Master the imperfect tense to describe ongoing, habitual, or background actions in the past. This week teaches you when to use imperfect versus preterite, allowing you to tell more complex, nuanced stories about your past experiences.
  Lessons:
    - Imperfect Tense Basics: Regular and Irregular Forms: Introduction to the imperfect tense for describing habitual actions, ongoing states, and background descriptions in past Spanish.
    - Imperfect: Habitual, Ongoing, and Descriptive Past: - Understand imperfect use for repeated habits, ongoing actions, and descriptions in the past
- Distinguish between momentary actions (preterite) and continuous/habitual actions (imperfect)
- Complete fill-in-the-blank exercises showing imperfect in context (childhood habits, daily routines, descriptions)
- Write sentences describing what you used to do or what was happening at a specific time
    - Preterite vs. Imperfect: Crucial Distinctions: - Compare preterite (completed actions) and imperfect (background/ongoing actions)

<plan>

## What this lesson actually needs to teach

Stripping the activity suggestions from the description, the learning outcomes are:
1. Know all 6 present tense conjugations of ir (voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van) — which are fully irregular and must be memorised
2. Understand the ir + a + infinitive construction as the Spanish equivalent of "going to + verb"
3. Produce sentences using this structure to talk about near-future plans across all persons

This is Week 4, Lesson 1 — the week's pivot from past tenses to future expressions. The student has fully mastered preterite and imperfect. This is their first exposure to anything future-facing. The later lessons this week will cover the formal future tense and the nuanced distinction between ir + a and future tense, so this lesson should build deep fluency with ir + a without worrying about that contrast yet. It should leave the student able to hold a natural conversation about near-future plans.

## Section split

There are two genuinely distinct things to learn here:
1. **The ir conjugations themselves** — these are fully irregular, need to be memorised cold, and are the mechanical prerequisite for everything else
2. **The full ir + a + infinitive structure in real communicative use** — once the conjugations are in place, the student needs to use the construction naturally to express plans, intentions, and predictions

These deserve separate sections. Section 1 is necessarily short — the 6 forms are not complex — but without drilling them to automaticity first, Section 2 collapses. Combining them into one section would rush past the conjugation drilling before the forms are secure.

---

## Section 1: Ir Conjugations and the Near Future Structure

**Section goal:** The student can correctly produce all 6 present tense conjugations of ir and slot them into the ir + a + infinitive frame.

**Units:**

1. **Explanation** — Introduce ir as a fully irregular verb, present all 6 conjugations, then immediately show the ir + a + infinitive structure with rich examples (Voy a comer, Vamos a salir, Ella va a estudiar). This section establishes both the forms and the structural frame.

2. **Flashcard** — Double-sided deck covering ir → to go, then all 6 conjugations paired with their pronoun+meaning (voy → I go, vas → you go, etc.). These are genuinely new forms the student must internalise.

3. **Word match** — Pronouns on the left, ir conjugations on the right (with distractors). Immediate recall practice after flashcards.

4. **Listening word order** — Hear short ir + a + infinitive sentences and arrange scrambled words. Introduces audio before production exercises and reinforces both form and sentence structure simultaneously.

5. **Fill in blanks (MC)** — Sentences with the ir form blanked out, choosing the correct conjugation. Guided production within the full ir + a + infinitive frame. The infinitive and subject are given — student only has to supply the correct ir form.

6. **Write in blanks** — Same concept but typed — student must recall and produce the correct ir form without options. This is the section's production exit — the student must demonstrate they've internalised the conjugations.

---

## Section 2: Expressing Near Future Plans with Ir + A + Infinitive

**Section goal:** The student can use ir + a + infinitive fluently and naturally to talk about real near-future plans across different subjects and contexts.

**Units:**

1. **Explanation** — Shift focus from form to meaning and usage. Explain when ir + a + infinitive is used (immediate/definite plans, "going to"), show it in varied contexts (tonight, this weekend, next week, life plans), highlight that only the ir form changes — the infinitive never conjugates. Give 8-10 natural example sentences.

2. **Conversation** — Two characters (friends or flatmates) discussing their weekend plans using ir + a + infinitive across multiple subjects. This shows the structure in natural spoken dialogue before the student has to produce it themselves.

3. **Listening fill** — Hear ir + a + infinitive sentences; the ir form is blanked in the text. Student selects which form they heard from 3 options (e.g. voy / va / vamos). Tests auditory discrimination of the conjugation endings.

4. **Dialogue fill** — User completes a dialogue about upcoming plans. The other character asks questions that elicit specific ir forms; user selects the correct ir + a + infinitive construction from options. Bridges from recognition to production in a conversational frame.

5. **Word order** — Scrambled ir + a + infinitive sentences to assemble. Reinforces that the infinitive always comes after "a" and never changes form.

6. **Translation** — 5 English "going to" sentences → Spanish ir + a + infinitive. Full production with no scaffolding. Varied subjects and a range of common infinitives (salir, comprar, ver, estudiar, viajar). This is the hardest exercise in this section.

7. **Writing practice** — Open prompt: write 5–7 sentences about plans for this week or weekend using ir + a + infinitive with at least 4 different subjects and 5 different infinitives.

---

## Review

The review tests everything together: can the student use the full ir + a + infinitive system across all persons, in mixed contexts, with no scaffolding? It introduces nothing new — only integrates.

**Units:**

1. **Write in blanks (cohesive story)** — A short narrative (someone describing their family's plans for an upcoming trip) with the ir forms blanked out throughout. Student types the correct conjugation. Mixed subjects across all 6 pronouns, no hints — context and subject determine the form.

2. **Correction** — 6 sentences with highlighted errors: wrong ir conjugation, conjugated infinitive (e.g. *voy a como* instead of voy a comer), missing "a," and swapped person. Student rewrites the highlighted error. Tests whether they understand the structure's rules deeply enough to spot violations.

3. **Translation** — 5 more complex English → Spanish sentences. These use compound subjects, negative forms (no voy a...), and question forms (¿Vas a...?). Full production.

4. **Dictation** — 4 ir + a + infinitive sentences spoken at natural speed. Student transcribes. Tests whether they can hear and reproduce the structure accurately in writing, including accent marks on the ir conjugations and the infinitives.

---

## Listening coverage check

- Section 1: listening_word_order ✓
- Section 2: listening_fill ✓, conversation ✓, dialogue_fill ✓
- Review: dictation ✓

Full coverage across all three listening types. Structural rules all satisfied. No back-to-back flashcards. MCQ not used for fill-in-the-blank tasks. Every section starts with explanation. Review has no explanation.

</plan>

<lesson>

<context>This lesson introduces the ir + a + infinitive structure — the Spanish equivalent of "going to" — for talking about near-future plans and intentions. <section name="Ir Conjugations and the Near Future Structure">

  <unit type="explanation" name="Ir: Conjugations and the Near Future Frame">
    Explain the present tense conjugations of ir (to go). It is fully irregular — none of its forms follow a predictable pattern — so the six forms must be memorised: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van. Show each form paired with its subject pronoun. Then immediately introduce the ir + a + infinitive construction: [conjugated ir] + a + [infinitive]. Explain that this is the Spanish equivalent of "going to [do something]" in English — used to talk about plans, intentions, and things that are about to happen. Show at least 8 varied examples covering all 6 persons: Voy a comer, Vas a llamar a tu madre, Él va a trabajar mañana, Vamos a ver una película esta noche, Vais a llegar tarde, Ellos van a viajar a México. Emphasise that ONLY the ir form changes to match the subject — the infinitive that follows always stays in its base form and never conjugates.
  </unit>

  <unit type="flashcard" name="Ir Conjugations">
    Generate a double-sided flashcard deck of 7 cards covering the verb ir and all its present tense conjugations. Example cards:
    ir → to go
    voy → I go / I am going
    vas → you go / you are going (informal singular)
    va → he/she/it goes / is going
    vamos → we go / we are going
    vais → you all go / you are going (Spain, plural)
    van → they go / they are going
  </unit>

  <unit type="word_match" name="Ir Pronoun Match">
    Test whether the student can match subject pronouns to the correct conjugation of ir. Explanation: "Match each subject pronoun to the correct form of ir." Left column "Pronoun" contains 5 items: yo, tú, él/ella, nosotros, ellos/ellas. Right column "Ir Form" contains 8 items including the correct matches and distractors: voy, vas, va, vamos, vais, van, iba, fue.
  </unit>

  <unit type="listening_word_order" name="Hear and Build Ir Sentences">
    Generate 4 listening word order exercises. Each sentence uses ir + a + infinitive with a different subject pronoun. Sentences should be 6–9 words long. The student listens to the complete sentence, then arranges the scrambled words to match. For example:
    "Voy a estudiar esta noche."
    "Ella va a cocinar la cena."
    "Nosotros vamos a salir mañana."
    "Ellos van a visitar a sus abuelos."
  </unit>

  <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Choose the Right Ir Form">
    Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence in the ir + a + infinitive structure, with the conjugated ir form blanked out. The subject pronoun is always explicit in the sentence. Include the hint "ir" underneath each blank. 3 distractors per blank — all other conjugations of ir. For example:
    1. "Yo [voy] a comprar pan en la tienda." Hint: "ir". Distractors: vas, va, vamos.
    2. "Tú [vas] a llegar tarde esta noche." Hint: "ir". Distractors: voy, van, vais.
    3. "Nosotros [vamos] a cenar en casa de mis padres." Hint: "ir". Distractors: voy, van, va.
    4. "Ella [va] a empezar su nuevo trabajo el lunes." Hint: "ir". Distractors: voy, vamos, vas.
    5. "Vosotros [vais] a necesitar más tiempo." Hint: "ir". Distractors: van, va, vamos.
    6. "Ellos [van] a tomar el tren de las ocho." Hint: "ir". Distractors: vamos, vais, va.
  </unit>

  <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Produce the Ir Form">
    Generate 6 isolated write-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence uses ir + a + infinitive with the conjugated ir form blanked out. The subject is explicit in each sentence. Include the hint "ir" for each blank so the student knows which verb to conjugate — they must recall the correct form without options. For example:
    1. "Yo [voy] a llamar a mi amigo esta tarde." Hint: "ir".
    2. "Tú [vas] a necesitar un abrigo esta noche." Hint: "ir".
    3. "Mi hermana [va] a vivir en Barcelona el próximo año." Hint: "ir".
    4. "Nosotros [vamos] a hacer una pausa ahora." Hint: "ir".
    5. "Vosotros [vais] a disfrutar mucho del viaje." Hint: "ir".
    6. "Mis padres [van] a comprar un coche nuevo." Hint: "ir".
  </unit>

</section>

<section name="Expressing Near Future Plans with Ir + A + Infinitive">

  <unit type="explanation" name="Talking About Plans: Ir + A in Context">
    Explain that ir + a + infinitive is the most common and natural way to talk about near-future plans and intentions in Spanish — both in everyday conversation and in more formal situations. It covers anything from "I'm going to eat in a minute" to "We're going to move to a new city next year." Show how it appears across varied time frames: esta noche, mañana, el próximo fin de semana, el año que viene. Provide 10 rich example sentences across all 6 persons, mixing different infinitives and topics (travel, food, work, study, social plans). Include 2–3 negative examples (No voy a salir esta noche, No van a poder venir) and one question form (¿Vas a venir a la fiesta?). Highlight that the infinitive following "a" never changes regardless of the subject — only the ir form shifts.
