lessonTitle: Complete Timeline Review: Past, Present, and Future Tenses
lessonOverview:
Lesson Title: Complete Timeline Review: Past, Present, and Future Tenses
Description: - Review present, preterite, imperfect, near future (ir + a), and future tense in context
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises spanning all tenses learned so far
- Identify tense choices in provided passages and explain reasoning
- Production: Write a 400-word piece covering a past event, current situation, and future plans, using appropriate tenses throughout
curriculumContext:
Month 1: Mastering Core Tenses and Accuracy
Month goal: This month focuses on solidifying your command of the present, preterite, and imperfect tenses with an emphasis on accuracy and natural usage. You'll practice common mistakes and build confidence in everyday conversations through targeted exercises that highlight when to use each tense correctly.

Previous week (Week 2): Preterite Tense Mastery and Regular Patterns
  Goal: Build confidence with the preterite tense by mastering regular patterns, understanding conjugation rules, and practicing common irregular forms. This week emphasizes the distinction between actions completed in the past and prepares you for combining tenses naturally.
  Lessons:
    - Preterite Regular Verbs: Foundations: - Learn regular preterite conjugations for -ar, -er, -ir verbs with clear pattern recognition
- Understand preterite endings and identify regular patterns (habé, comí, viví)
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises with high-frequency regular verbs in preterite
- Translate 10 English past tense sentences into Spanish using regular preterite verbs
    - Preterite Irregular Verbs: High-Frequency Forms: - Cover the most common irregular preterite verbs (ser/fue, ir/fue, tener/tuve, estar/estuve, hacer/hice, poder/pude, poner/puse)
- Understand irregular stem patterns and how to recognize them
- Practice conjugation through flashcard-style activities with context sentences
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises using high-frequency irregular preterites in realistic narratives
    - Preterite vs. Present Perfect: When to Use Each: - Distinguish between preterite (simple past) and present perfect (have done) in Spanish usage
- Understand regional differences (Spain vs. Latin America) in usage preferences
- Complete sentences choosing between preterite and present perfect forms
- Rewrite 8 sentences, switching between preterite and present perfect where appropriate
    - Preterite in Narrative and Personal Stories: - Apply preterite tense to storytelling and personal anecdotes (vacation, weekend, past event)
- Practice with narrative passages that use preterite for completed actions
- Fill-in-the-blank in short story excerpts using appropriate preterite forms
- Write a 150-word paragraph about something you did last weekend using accurate preterite tense
    - Preterite Mastery Review: Regular, Irregular, and Context: - Review all preterite concepts: regular patterns, common irregulars, and narrative usage
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises with preterite verbs in varied contexts
- Identify correct vs. incorrect preterite conjugations in 12-15 sentences
- Production: Write 300 words describing a past event, trip, or significant day using varied preterite verbs

Previous week (Week 3): Imperfect Tense and Past Continuous Action
  Goal: Master the imperfect tense to describe ongoing, habitual, or background actions in the past. This week teaches you when to use imperfect versus preterite, allowing you to tell more complex, nuanced stories about your past experiences.
  Lessons:
    - Imperfect Tense Basics: Regular and Irregular Forms: Introduction to the imperfect tense for describing habitual actions, ongoing states, and background descriptions in past Spanish.
    - Imperfect: Habitual, Ongoing, and Descriptive Past: - Understand imperfect use for repeated habits, ongoing actions, and descriptions in the past
- Distinguish between momentary actions (preterite) and continuous/habitual actions (imperfect)
- Complete fill-in-the-blank exercises showing imperfect in context (childhood habits, daily routines, descriptions)
- Write sentences describing what you used to do or what was happening at a specific time
    - Preterite vs. Imperfect: Crucial Distinctions: - Compare preterite (completed actions) and imperfect (background/ongoing actions)
- Practice with paired sentences showing the difference in meaning
- Rewrite sentences switching between preterite and imperfect to show meaning changes
- Complete exercises selecting the correct tense based on context clues in Spanish sentences
    - Imperfect in Storytelling: Past Narrative Layers: - Apply both imperfect and preterite together in complex narrative passages
- Practice with short stories that layer background (imperfect) with specific events (preterite)
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises in narrative contexts requiring both tenses
- Write a short story (150-200 words) about a memorable childhood moment using both tenses appropriately
    - Past Tenses Mastery Review: Preterite, Imperfect, and Narrative Cohesion: - Review preterite vs. imperfect distinctions, conjugations, and usage contexts
- Complete complex mixed exercises combining preterite, imperfect, and present perfect
- Identify tense choices in provided passages and explain why each tense was chosen
- Production: Write a 300-word personal narrative mixing preterite and imperfect naturally (e.g., describing a past experience with background details)

>> CURRENT WEEK (Week 4): Present and Future Plans: Combining Tenses
  Goal: Expand your range by mastering how to combine present and future expressions to discuss plans, intentions, and predictions. This week bridges past, present, and future storytelling, enabling you to have complete conversations about your life timeline.
  Lessons:
    - Near Future: Ir + A + Infinitive: - Learn the present tense ir + a + infinitive structure for discussing immediate future plans

<plan>

## Step 1: What does this lesson actually need to do?

The lesson description is a capstone review — it mentions "comprehensive mixed exercises spanning all tenses" and a 400-word production piece. Stripping out the activity suggestions, the genuine learning outcomes are:

1. Discriminate accurately between all five tenses covered in Month 1 so far: **present, preterite, imperfect, ir + a, and future**
2. Choose the correct tense based on contextual cues (completed action, ongoing/habitual past, background description, immediate plans, speculative/distant future)
3. Produce extended multi-tense writing that naturally flows across a full personal timeline

This is NOT an introduction to any new material. Everything has been learned. The lesson's job is integration and discrimination — making sure the student can switch fluidly between five tenses in one sustained context, not just perform each tense in isolation. The 400-word production piece is the highest-stakes task of the entire month so far.

Curriculum note: Week 5 jumps straight into the subjunctive. There is no more tense review coming. This is the final consolidation before a radically different grammar concept. The student needs to leave this lesson with genuine multi-tense fluency, not just loose familiarity.

## Step 2: Breaking into sections

Because this is a review lesson, the "learning sections" are organized by **contrast groups** rather than individual tenses. The student has studied each tense separately — the cognitive challenge now is discrimination when all five appear together.

Two natural groupings emerge:

**Section 1 — Past Tenses (Present, Preterite, Imperfect):** These three tenses form the core of Month 1 Weeks 1–3 and are the hardest to confuse. Preterite vs imperfect is the trickiest discrimination in Spanish for learners at this level. Present often bleeds into the mix when students describe habitual actions. The goal: student can choose correctly between all three in a mixed narrative without scaffolding.

**Section 2 — Future Tenses (Ir + A vs Future Tense):** These are this week's material and the contrast is more nuanced (immediate/definite vs speculative/distant). The goal: student can produce both future forms correctly and knows when to use each.

**Review — Full Timeline Integration:** All five tenses in one place. No new explanations — just the hardest version of what the sections practiced, plus the capstone 400-word writing task.

Reasons this split works pedagogically:
- Students need the past tenses reinforced before mixing in the futures (the past tenses are older, the futures are newer)
- Each group has a clean conceptual identity (past time vs future time)
- The review is genuinely integrative because it forces switching across BOTH groups simultaneously

## Step 3: Units per section

### Section 1: Past Tenses (Present, Preterite, Imperfect)
**Section goal:** Student can identify and produce the correct past/present tense form based solely on contextual cues, without options or scaffolding.

- **explanation** — Recap all three tenses: what each is for, the key contrasts (preterite = completed event, imperfect = ongoing/habitual/background, present = current/habitual state). Frame this as a reminder, not a re-teaching.
- **conversation** — Two characters telling the story of a past trip/event. Should demonstrate all three tenses in a natural narrative context (preterite for the story's events, imperfect for background/feelings/weather, present for habitual contrast). Students hear what natural multi-tense storytelling sounds like.
- **mcq** — Classify 6 sentences as preterite, imperfect, or present (same 3 options throughout). Exercises test conceptual discrimination — which tense is used here and why. NOT asking to fill a blank.
- **fill_in_blanks** — Cohesive story passage (~8 sentences). Blanks are conjugated verbs with 3–4 options per blank (correct form + plausible alternatives from other tenses). Infinitive hint visible since the form is already known; the test is tense choice.
- **write_in_blanks** — Second cohesive story passage. Student types the verb from scratch given only the infinitive as a hint. No options. Forces unscaffolded production.
- **correction** — Short passage with 5–6 highlighted tense errors (preterite where imperfect belongs or vice versa, or wrong conjugation within a tense). Students fix the highlighted words.
- **[listening_fill]** — 5 sentences. Audio plays the full sentence. Student identifies whether the verb they heard is preterite or imperfect (options are forms of the same verb in different tenses). Tests auditory discrimination of accent/ending differences (e.g. "habló" vs "hablaba").

### Section 2: Future Tenses (Ir + A vs Future Tense)
**Section goal:** Student can produce the correct future construction (ir + a or future tense) and can discriminate between them based on contextual cues.

*Why parallel structure differs from Section 1:* Section 1 covers tenses the student has practiced for 2+ weeks — error correction (correction unit) is appropriate because they should be beyond production and into self-editing. Section 2 covers tenses introduced this week — translation is more appropriate here because the student is still building production fluency and needs to generate these forms from English stimulus, not just edit them.

- **explanation** — Recap the contrast: ir + a = immediate, planned, certain near-future; future tense = more distant, speculative, predictive. Quick conjugation reference for ir in this construction and the future tense endings including common irregular stems.
- **conversation** — Two characters discussing weekend plans (ir + a) and long-term life goals (future tense). Natural integration of both forms in one conversation.
- **mcq** — Classify 6 sentences as ir + a or future tense (same 2 options throughout). Context-based — time markers and tone cues signal which form is appropriate.
- **fill_in_blanks** — 6 isolated sentences, each blanking one future construction. Options include the correct form plus plausible alternatives (wrong construction, wrong conjugation). Infinitive hint provided.
- **write_in_blanks** — 6 isolated sentences, student types the correct future construction given the infinitive and subject. Forces recall of both forms.
- **translation** — 5 English sentences using "going to" or "will." Student must choose which Spanish construction is appropriate and produce it fully. No hints.
- **[listening_fill]** — 5 sentences. Audio plays the full sentence. Student identifies whether they heard ir + a or future tense (options are both constructions of the same verb). Tests whether students can hear the phonetic difference between "voy a hablar" and "hablaré."

### Review
**Review goal:** Student can integrate all five tenses across a full personal timeline narrative and produce a 400-word extended piece.

- **fill_in_blanks** — One cohesive "full timeline" passage (~10 sentences) covering a past event, current life, and future plans. Blanks across all five tenses. Options per blank include plausible alternatives from other tenses. This is the first time all five tenses appear in a single passage.
- **word_order** — 5–6 scrambled sentences mixing all tenses. Forces the student to think about sentence structure and verb placement across different tense forms.
- **correction** — Mixed-tense passage (8 sentences) with 6–7 highlighted errors spanning all five tenses (wrong tense choice, wrong conjugation, wrong ir+a structure, etc.).
- **translation** — 5 English sentences mixing all tenses. Students must select the right tense AND produce the correct form from scratch. No hints.
- **[dictation]** — 4–5 sentences mixing all five tenses. Cohesive passage about someone's life timeline. Tests whether students can hear and write forms they've been producing all lesson.
- **live_conversation** — Student talks to an AI character (a Spanish-speaking friend they're catching up with after a long time). Objectives: describe something that happened last year, explain current life situation, say two things they're going to do soon, and say one thing they hope for the future. This is a rehearsal for the writing task — spoken, time-pressured, all five tenses required.
- **writing_practice** — 400-word personal narrative. Write about a meaningful past event, your current situation, and your future plans. Must use all five tenses. This is the capstone of the entire month.

## Step 4: Parallel structure check
- Section 1 and Section 2 are both recap sections for a tense contrast group. They share: explanation → conversation → mcq → fill_in_blanks → write_in_blanks → [different unit] → listening_fill. The sixth core unit differs: Section 1 uses correction (established tenses, student should self-edit), Section 2 uses translation (newer tenses, student needs production practice). This is a content-justified difference.

## Step 5: Supplementary units
- Section 1 gets listening_fill (preterite vs imperfect auditory discrimination — critical because these are the two tenses that differ by subtle endings/accents)
- Section 2 gets listening_fill (ir + a vs future tense — students need to hear whether someone says "voy a comer" vs "comeré")
- Review gets dictation (the student has reached full production confidence in the review; dictation is a cognitively demanding but accessible listen-and-write task here)

</plan>

```xml
<lesson>
  <context>This is the capstone review of everything you've learned in Month 1 so far. You'll bring together the present, preterite, imperfect, ir + a, and future tenses — first by sharpening your discrimination within each contrast group, then by combining all five tenses in a single timeline narrative and a 400-word extended writing task.</context>

  <section name="Past Tenses Recap: Present, Preterite, and Imperfect">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Three Tenses, One Story">Recap the three Spanish tenses used to talk about past and present time: present tense (habitual/current states and actions), preterite (completed, bounded past actions — the events that drive a story forward), and imperfect (ongoing, habitual, or background past — descriptions, feelings, weather, what was happening when something else occurred). Frame this as a reminder of distinctions the student already knows: the key is that all three can appear in the same story, each doing a different job. Use a short example passage that contains all three tenses to illustrate how they layer together naturally in narrative. Emphasise the context clues (time markers, habitual signals, narrative event markers) that signal which tense belongs where.</unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Un fin de semana en Sevilla">Generate a single conversation (10–12 lines) between two friends — Lucía and Marcos — catching up after a trip. Lucía has just returned from a weekend in Sevilla. The dialogue should naturally demonstrate all three tenses: preterite for the specific completed events of the trip (visited a cathedral, ate at a specific restaurant, met someone), imperfect for background description and ongoing states (the weather was beautiful, the streets were full of people, she used to visit as a child), and present tense for habitual/current contrast (she says she always loves Sevilla, Marcos says he usually prefers Madrid). The conversation should feel like a real, flowing exchange — not a grammar demonstration. 2 characters only.</unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Which Tense Is It?">Classify each sentence as preterite, imperfect, or present tense. Explanation: "Decide which tense is being used in each sentence." Generate 6 exercises. Each exercise is a single Spanish sentence in one of the three tenses. The same 3 options appear for every exercise: a) Preterite b) Imperfect c) Present. Choose sentences that illustrate genuine usage contrasts — e.g. one preterite with a completed event marker (ayer, el lunes pasado), one imperfect with a habitual marker (siempre, todos los días when referring to the past), one imperfect describing an ongoing past state, one present for a current/habitual fact, and so on. Avoid sentences where two answers could be plausible — each sentence should have one clear answer.</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Una tarde en el parque">Generate 8 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story. The story is about a person remembering an afternoon in a park from their childhood and how one specific event changed things. Each sentence has one verb blanked. The blank tests tense choice: preterite, imperfect, or present tense. Include the infinitive form as a hint under each blank. Provide 3 options per blank: the correct form, a plausible alternative from a different tense of the same verb, and one more distractor (wrong person or tense). For example: "Cuando era niño, [jugaba] en este parque todos los días." Hint: "jugar". Distractors: jugué, juego. The story should flow naturally and require a genuine mix of all three tenses — background description in imperfect, key events in preterite, and present tense for the narrator's current reflective state.</unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="El día que todo cambió">Generate 8 write-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story. A different story from the fill_in_blanks — this one is about someone describing a significant day that changed their career path. Each sentence has one verb blanked. No options — the student types the answer. Include the infinitive as a hint for every blank. The story must require a natural mix of all three tenses (imperfect for what life was like before, preterite for the specific events of that day, present for how things are now). For example: "Antes de ese día, [trabajaba] en una oficina aburrida." Hint: "trabajar". "Pero una mañana, mi jefe me [llamó] a su despacho." Hint: "llamar".</unit>

    <unit type="correction" name="Fix the Tense Errors">Generate 6 isolated correction exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence with one verb form highlighted as incorrect. The error is always a tense confusion: either preterite used where imperfect is needed, imperfect used where preterite is needed, or present used where preterite/imperfect is needed. The student must rewrite the highlighted word with the correct form. Errors should reflect realistic learner mistakes — e.g. using imperfect for a clearly completed narrative event, or using preterite for a habitual past action. For example: "Ayer yo ["hablaba": "hablé"] con mi jefa durante una hora." (preterite needed for completed, bounded event). "De niño, siempre ["fui": "iba"] al colegio a pie." (imperfect needed for habitual past).</unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Preterite or Imperfect — Hear the Difference">Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises targeting auditory discrimination between preterite and imperfect. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the verb blanked. The student selects which form they heard from 3 options — always forms of the SAME verb in different tenses (preterite, imperfect, and one more alternative such as present or future). The sentences should feature verbs whose preterite and imperfect forms differ by subtle accent or ending changes, making listening discrimination genuinely challenging (e.g. "habló" vs "hablaba", "comió" vs "comía", "vivió" vs "vivía", "estuvo" vs "estaba"). For example: Audio: "Cuando era pequeña, vivía en un pueblo tranquilo." Text: "Cuando era pequeña, [______] en un pueblo tranquilo." Options: vivía, vivió, vive.</unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Future Tenses Recap: Ir + A and Future Tense">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Near Future vs Future Tense">Recap the two ways Spanish expresses future time: ir + a + infinitive (voy a, vas a, va a, vamos a, vais a, van a) for immediate, definite, or planned near-future actions — things that feel certain and close; and the future tense (hablaré, comerás, vivirá…) for more distant, speculative, or predictive statements — predictions, promises, things that might happen. Include a quick conjugation reference for both constructions, including the common irregular future stems (tener/tendr-, poder/podr-, saber/sabr-, hacer/har-, decir/dir-, venir/vendr-, salir/saldr-). Use paired example sentences to show the same verb in both constructions and how the nuance shifts — e.g. "Voy a llamarle esta tarde" (definite plan) vs "Le llamaré cuando pueda" (less certain, more speculative). Keep the explanation concise — this is a reminder, not a first introduction.</unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Planes y sueños">Generate a single conversation (10–12 lines) between two friends — Elena and Pablo — talking about their near-term weekend plans and their long-term life ambitions. The dialogue should naturally use ir + a for concrete, imminent plans (going to the cinema tonight, going to visit a friend this weekend) and future tense for more distant or speculative goals (will probably move to another city, will hopefully have their own business one day, will study more when they have time). The contrast between the two forms should feel natural, not forced — the characters should sound like real people talking about their lives. 2 characters only.</unit>

Sentences should represent genuine usage contrasts — e.g. sentences with esta tarde/mañana/esta semana tend to be ir + a; sentences with algún día/cuando sea mayor/en el futuro tend to use future tense; weather or news predictions (Mañana lloverá) use future tense; announced plans (Esta noche voy a estudiar) use ir + a. Include a good mix and avoid ambiguous cases.</unit>

Each sentence has one future construction blanked — either the full ir + a + infinitive phrase (e.g. "voy a estudiar") or a future tense conjugated verb (e.g. "estudiaré"). Provide the infinitive as a hint underneath the blank. Provide 3 options per blank: the correct form, the alternative future construction of the same verb (if correct is ir + a, distractor is future tense form; if correct is future tense, distractor is ir + a form), and one more distractor (wrong person or irregular stem error). Context in each sentence should clearly signal which construction is appropriate (time markers, tone). For example: "Esta noche [voy a cenar] con mis padres en casa." Hint: "cenar". Distractors: cenaré, vas a cenar.</unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Tus planes y tus sueños">Generate 6 isolated write-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence has one future construction blanked — either ir + a + infinitive or future tense. The student types the full answer with no options. Provide the infinitive as a hint, along with the subject if it is not explicit in the sentence. For example: "Este fin de semana [vamos a visitar] a mis abuelos." Hint: "nosotros – visitar". "Algún día [tendré] mi propio negocio." Hint: "tener". Include a mix of regular and irregular future stems across the 6 sentences.</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Going To or Will?">Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the user to translate into Spanish. (future tense, distant aspiration) 3. "We're going to watch a film tonight." (ir + a) 4. "She will probably be a doctor." (future tense, prediction) 5. "Are you going to study tomorrow?" (ir + a, concrete near-future question)</unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Near Future or Future Tense — Hear the Difference">Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the future verb or ir + a phrase blanked. The student selects which form they heard from 3 options. Options should always include: the correct ir + a form, the correct future tense form of the same verb, and one other distractor (wrong person). The goal is auditory discrimination between the rhythmically different "voy a + infinitive" pattern and the single-word future tense ending. For example: Audio: "Mañana por la mañana voy a salir temprano." Text: "Mañana por la mañana [______] temprano." Options: voy a salir, saldré, vas a salir.</unit>

  </section>

  <review>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Una vida entera">Generate 10 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a single cohesive "full timeline" passage. The passage is a first-person narrative covering: a meaningful event from the past (using preterite and imperfect), the narrator's current life and situation (using present tense), and their near and distant future plans (using ir + a and future tense). Every sentence has one verb blanked. All five tenses must appear across the 10 sentences. Include the infinitive as a hint for each blank. Provide 3–4 options per blank including the correct form and plausible alternatives from other tenses of the same verb — the student must choose based on contextual understanding of the timeline.     <unit type="word_order" name="Timeline Sentences">Generate 6 word order exercises. Describe one specific thing that happened to you in the past two years using preterite and imperfect (e.g. a trip, a job change, a life event). 2. output:
```
<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to do

This is Lesson 1 of Week 5, the first lesson the student encounters on the subjunctive mood. The title and description are clear: this is a **conceptual orientation** lesson. The student should finish understanding *why* the subjunctive exists in Spanish, *how* it differs from the indicative, and *what kinds of phrases/contexts trigger it*.

Critically, the student does NOT yet know how to conjugate in the subjunctive — that comes in Lesson 2. So this lesson cannot reasonably demand production of conjugated subjunctive forms. However, that doesn't mean the lesson has no production dimension. The "production" here is recognizing triggers, classifying contexts, and constructing the overall sentence frame (trigger phrase + que + clause) — a structural competence that underpins everything in Weeks 5–6.

**Learning outcomes (actual):**
- Understand what the subjunctive mood is and how it differs from the indicative conceptually
- Understand the basic "main clause + que + subordinate clause" sentence architecture
- Recognize the major trigger categories: desire/wishes, emotion, doubt/denial, recommendations, impersonal expressions
- Identify specific trigger phrases and classify what category they belong to
- Discriminate whether a given sentence context calls for subjunctive or indicative (without needing to conjugate)

**Activity suggestions from the description to discard:** None explicitly listed — the description is concise and conceptual. Good.

## Step 2: Breaking into sections

**Section 1: The Subjunctive Mood — What It Is and Why It Exists**
This section builds the conceptual foundation. The student needs to understand the indicative/subjunctive distinction (factual/certain vs. uncertain/desired/subjective) and the basic structural architecture (main clause + que + subordinate clause). Without this, trigger categories in Section 2 are just a list to memorize with no logic behind them.
Goal: *Be able to classify whether a sentence context is factual/certain (indicative) or non-factual/uncertain/subjective (subjunctive) and explain why.*

**Section 2: Trigger Categories and Phrases — Recognizing the Signals**
Once the conceptual logic is clear, this section maps that logic onto specific trigger phrase categories and vocabulary. The student learns the main families of triggers (desire, emotion, doubt, recommendation, impersonal expressions) and the actual phrases that belong to each. This is the recognition competence the rest of Week 5 builds on.
Goal: *Be able to recognize a trigger phrase, classify it into its category, and identify whether it signals subjunctive or indicative.*

Two sections is right for this lesson. There's no third topic to introduce — the lesson is specifically foundational/conceptual and conjugation details are deliberately deferred to Lessons 2–4. Adding a third section risks bleeding into Lesson 2's territory.

**Parallel structure check:** Both sections follow a similar arc — explain the concept, show it in use, recognize it, classify it. Section 2 adds flashcards because it introduces specific vocabulary (trigger phrases) that Section 1 doesn't need. Otherwise the exercise ramps are comparable. No mismatch.

## Step 3: Units per section

**Section 1:**
- `explanation` — What the subjunctive is, the indicative vs. subjunctive distinction (certain/factual vs. uncertain/desired/subjective), the que-clause architecture. Show parallel sentences with indicative vs. subjunctive contexts side by side (even without full conjugations — use brackets for the unknown verb to show structure).
- `conversation` — Two characters naturally using trigger phrases in real dialogue. This is high-value here because hearing the subjunctive in real exchanges gives the student a model of how it actually sounds and functions before any exercises.
- `mcq` — 6 exercises: classify each sentence situation as indicative-context or subjunctive-context based on meaning alone. Same 2 options throughout. Tests conceptual discrimination.
- `word_order` — Unscramble full sentences using the que-clause structure. Even though students can't conjugate the subjunctive verb yet, they can see the structure and unscramble it (the subjunctive verb form is provided in the tiles). This reinforces the architecture.
- (Supplementary added in Step 5)

**Section 2:**
- `explanation` — The five main trigger categories with example phrases and one-sentence explanation of why each triggers subjunctive. Include a clear list/table format.
- `flashcard` — 10 trigger phrases, double-sided: Spanish phrase ↔ English meaning. This is genuine vocabulary introduction.
- `word_match` — Match 5 trigger phrases to their category (desire / emotion / doubt / recommendation / impersonal). Left = phrases, right = categories with distractors.
- `fill_in_blanks` — 6 sentences where the student selects the correct trigger phrase to complete the sentence meaningfully. Tests whether they understand what each trigger phrase means and when to use it.
- `translation` — 4 exercises: translate English sentences into Spanish, producing only the trigger phrase + que structure. E.g., "I want her to study" → "Quiero que ___." The student produces the trigger phrase; the subjunctive verb is left as a blank placeholder. This is appropriate production given where the student is in the curriculum.
- (Supplementary added in Step 5)

## Step 4: Review section

The review integrates both sections: conceptual discrimination (indicative vs. subjunctive context) + trigger phrase recognition and classification. No explanation. The review should mix trigger phrases from all categories and test whether the student can discriminate and apply what they've learned across both sections together.

Units:
- `word_match` — 5 trigger phrases → indicative or subjunctive? (Some phrases like "sé que" and "creo que" take indicative; subjunctive triggers contrast with them.) Tests discrimination.
- `mcq` — 6 sentences: classify as indicative context or subjunctive context. These are more complex/ambiguous than Section 1's MCQ — they include both confident assertions (creo que, sé que) and trigger phrases, requiring the student to discriminate carefully.
- `fill_in_blanks` — 5 sentences (cohesive passage) where the student chooses between subjunctive-triggering phrase or indicative phrase. Integrates both sections in narrative context.
- `translation` — 4 exercises: English → Spanish trigger phrase + que structure. Slightly more complex than Section 2's translation, mixing categories.
- (Supplementary added in Step 5)

## Step 5: Supplementary listening/dialogue units

**Section 1:** Add `listening_word_order` — hear full que-clause sentences and reconstruct them. Low-cost, high-value for introducing the *sound* of subjunctive sentence structure before the student can produce it.

**Section 2:** Add `listening_fill` — hear sentences with the trigger phrase spoken aloud and discriminate between similar-sounding trigger phrases (e.g. "espero que" vs. "creo que"). This targets auditory discrimination of the very phrases that are the lesson's core vocabulary.

**Review:** Add `dictation` — 4 sentences using trigger phrases from various categories. The student has now been exposed to these phrases multiple times and can transcribe them. Tests whether they can hear and reproduce the trigger + que structure accurately including accents and spelling.

## Structural checks

- Every section starts with `explanation` ✓
- No back-to-back flashcard units ✓
- No MCQ used to fill sentence blanks ✓
- Flashcard used only for direct translations ✓
- Listening/dialogue present: listening_word_order, listening_fill, dictation ✓
- Review has no explanation ✓
- Max 1 grammar concept per section ✓
- Production increases through the lesson: classification → recognition → structural production (trigger phrase translation) ✓

</plan>

```xml
<lesson>
  <context>This lesson introduces the subjunctive mood — what it is, why Spanish uses it, and how to spot the phrases that trigger it. You won't be conjugating subjunctive verbs yet (that comes in the next lesson), but by the end you'll understand the logic behind the subjunctive and be able to recognize the key trigger phrases that signal when it's needed.</context>

  <section name="The Subjunctive Mood — What It Is and Why It Exists">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Indicative vs. Subjunctive — The Core Distinction">
      Explain the conceptual purpose of the subjunctive mood in Spanish. Cover the following:
      1. Spanish has two main moods: the indicative (used for facts, certainties, and objective statements) and the subjunctive (used for wishes, doubts, emotions, recommendations, and uncertain or subjective situations).
      2. The indicative reports reality as the speaker sees it. The subjunctive expresses how the speaker *feels about* or *relates to* a situation — it carries subjectivity, uncertainty, or personal will.
      3. Show 3-4 paired examples contrasting indicative and subjunctive contexts in English first, then in Spanish, to make the logic visible. For example: "I know she studies hard" (fact → indicative) vs. "I want her to study hard" (wish → subjunctive). Provide the Spanish with the subjunctive verb placed in brackets and labelled as [subjunctive form to be learned in the next lesson] so the student can see the structure without needing to produce it yet.
      4. Introduce the essential sentence architecture: **main clause (indicative verb) + que + subordinate clause (subjunctive verb)**. Show this with a labelled diagram-style example (e.g. "Quiero [main: indicative] / que / ella estudie [subordinate: subjunctive]").
      5. Emphasise that subjunctive almost always requires two different subjects (the person wanting/doubting/recommending is different from the person performing the action). Briefly contrast with the infinitive construction used when subjects are the same (e.g. "Quiero estudiar" — same subject, no que needed).
      Keep the tone reassuring — frame subjunctive not as a complex exception but as a logical tool Spanish uses to signal when a speaker is expressing something personal or uncertain rather than stating a fact.
    </unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Wishes, Plans, and Doubts — Subjunctive in Real Dialogue">
      Generate a single conversation (8-10 lines) between two characters: Sofía (28, enthusiastic, planning a trip) and Andrés (30, cautious, sceptical about the plan). They are friends discussing a possible group trip to Peru. The conversation should naturally use 5-6 subjunctive trigger phrases in context — things like "espero que...", "quiero que...", "no creo que...", "es importante que...", "ojalá que...", "te recomiendo que..." — without any grammar notes or annotations. The dialogue should feel like a real, relaxed conversation. Both characters should have roughly equal turns. The subjunctive verb forms that follow these trigger phrases should be present (students will learn to conjugate them in the next lesson — they're just hearing the structure here).
    </unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Fact or Feeling? Classify the Context">
      Generate 6 classification exercises. The student reads a short English sentence or situation description and decides whether it is expressing a fact/certainty (Indicative context) or a wish/doubt/emotion/recommendation (Subjunctive context). The same 2 options apply to every exercise: a) Indicative context b) Subjunctive context. The prompt for each exercise is a single English sentence or situation — not a Spanish sentence. This tests conceptual understanding of when subjunctive logic applies, before any conjugation is introduced. The explanation shown to the user: "Decide whether each situation expresses a fact or certainty (indicative) or a wish, doubt, emotion, or recommendation (subjunctive)." Example exercises:
      — "She knows that her friend lives in Madrid." → Indicative context
      — "He hopes that she arrives on time." → Subjunctive context
      — "It's important that you rest today." → Subjunctive context
      — "I believe that the meeting is at three." → Indicative context
      — "They doubt that we'll finish in time." → Subjunctive context
      — "We see that the door is open." → Indicative context
      Generate 6 similar exercises covering a mix of desire, doubt, emotion, recommendation, and impersonal expression contexts alongside clear indicative fact contexts.
    </unit>

    <unit type="word_order" name="Build the Que-Clause Structure">
      Generate 5 word order exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence using the main clause + que + subordinate clause structure. The subjunctive verb form is already given in the tiles — students just need to assemble the correct order. Sentences should use simple vocabulary the student already knows. Include the English translation for each sentence so the student can verify meaning. Sentences should be 7-10 words and drawn from the trigger categories of desire, hope, recommendation, and impersonal expressions. Example sentences:
      1. "Quiero que mi amigo venga a la fiesta." (I want my friend to come to the party.)
      2. "Es importante que estudies todos los días." (It's important that you study every day.)
      3. "Espero que tengas un buen viaje." (I hope you have a good trip.)
      4. "Te recomiendo que pruebes la comida local." (I recommend you try the local food.)
      5. "No creo que llueva mañana." (I don't think it will rain tomorrow.)
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_word_order" name="Hear and Reconstruct the Subjunctive Structure">
      Generate 5 listening word order exercises. Each sentence uses the main clause + que + subordinate clause structure and features a subjunctive trigger phrase. The student listens to the complete spoken sentence and then arranges the shuffled word tiles into the correct order. Sentences should be 7-10 words. Focus on clear, natural sentences using vocabulary the student already knows. Sentences should use different trigger categories across the set. Example sentences:
      "Quiero que ella hable con el profesor."
      "Es necesario que bebas mucha agua."
      "Espero que podamos vernos pronto."
      "Dudamos que lleguen a tiempo."
      "Te sugiero que descanses esta noche."
    </unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Trigger Categories — Recognizing What Signals the Subjunctive">

    <unit type="explanation" name="The Five Trigger Categories">
      Explain the five main categories of subjunctive triggers in Spanish. For each category, give the logic (why it triggers subjunctive), 3-4 key example phrases, and one full example sentence showing the structure. The five categories are:
      1. **Desire and Will** — The speaker wants someone else to do something. Key phrases: querer que, desear que, esperar que, preferir que, pedir que. Example: "Quiero que vengas." (I want you to come.)
      2. **Emotion** — The speaker has a feeling about what someone does. Key phrases: alegrarse de que, temer que, sorprender que, gustar que, sentir que. Example: "Me alegra que estés aquí." (I'm glad you're here.)
      3. **Doubt and Denial** — The speaker is uncertain or negates a belief. Key phrases: dudar que, no creer que, no estar seguro/a de que, no es verdad que. Contrast: affirmative creer que / saber que take the indicative — make this clear. Example: "Dudo que lleguen hoy." (I doubt they'll arrive today.)
      4. **Recommendations and Suggestions** — The speaker advises someone to do something. Key phrases: recomendar que, sugerir que, aconsejar que, es mejor que. Example: "Te recomiendo que pruebes el ceviche." (I recommend you try the ceviche.)
      5. **Impersonal Expressions** — An impersonal statement evaluates or judges a situation. Key phrases: es importante que, es necesario que, es bueno que, es extraño que, conviene que. Contrast: es verdad que / es obvio que take the indicative. Example: "Es importante que llegues a tiempo." (It's important that you arrive on time.)
      Present these as a clear structured list. Emphasise that the common thread is that the speaker is not simply stating a fact — they are expressing a personal stance, value judgement, or uncertainty toward someone else's action.
    </unit>

    <unit type="flashcard" name="Key Trigger Phrases">
      Generate a double-sided flashcard deck of 10 cards covering the most important subjunctive trigger phrases across all five categories. Each card is a direct translation pair: Spanish trigger phrase on one side, English equivalent on the other. Example cards:
      Quiero que → I want (someone) to
      Espero que → I hope that
      Dudo que → I doubt that
      No creo que → I don't think that
      Es importante que → It's important that
      Te recomiendo que → I recommend that you
      Me alegra que → I'm glad that
      Es necesario que → It's necessary that
      Prefiero que → I prefer that
      Ojalá que → I hope that / Hopefully
      Distribute cards across all five trigger categories. Double-sided so the student is tested in both directions.
    </unit>

    <unit type="word_match" name="Trigger Phrases to Categories">
      Match subjunctive trigger phrases to their category. Explanation: "Match each trigger phrase to the category it belongs to." Left column "Trigger Phrase" contains 5 Spanish phrases. Right column "Category" contains 6 entries including one distractor category. Every item in both columns must be 1-3 words maximum — for the left column, use very short phrases (e.g. "Dudo que", "Es mejor que", "Me alegra que"). For the right column, use single category labels: Desire, Emotion, Doubt, Recommendation, Impersonal, Certainty (the distractor — this one doesn't trigger subjunctive).
      Left column (5 items): Dudo que, Es mejor que, Me alegra que, Quiero que, Es necesario que.
      Right column (6 items): Desire, Emotion, Doubt, Recommendation, Impersonal, Certainty.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Choose the Right Trigger Phrase">
      Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence where the trigger phrase is blanked out. The student selects the correct trigger phrase from a word pool. The sentences should make the appropriate category of trigger clear from context — the student must understand the meaning of the trigger phrases to choose correctly. No hints — context alone determines the right answer. 2-3 distractors per blank using trigger phrases from different categories that would be grammatically plausible but semantically wrong in context. Sentences should include the subjunctive verb already in place (students don't need to conjugate — they're completing the trigger phrase only).

      Example format:
      1. "____________ que llegues a tiempo, la reunión es muy importante." — Correct: Es importante que. Distractors: No creo que, Me alegra que.
      2. "____________ que vengas a mi fiesta — ¡va a ser increíble!" — Correct: Espero que. Distractors: Dudo que, Es necesario que.
      3. "____________ que el vuelo salga sin retraso, el aeropuerto estaba lleno." — Correct: Dudo que. Distractors: Quiero que, Es mejor que.
      4. "____________ que descanses antes del examen." — Correct: Te recomiendo que. Distractors: Me alegra que, Espero que.
      5. "____________ que estés aquí — te echábamos de menos." — Correct: Me alegra que. Distractors: Dudo que, Es necesario que.
      6. "____________ que estudies esta semana, el examen es el viernes." — Correct: Es mejor que. Distractors: Me alegra que, Quiero que.
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Trigger Phrase">
      Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the trigger phrase blanked out. The student selects which trigger phrase they heard from 3 options. Options should be trigger phrases that sound similar in structure or length to make the audio discrimination meaningful (e.g. "Espero que" vs "Dudo que", or "Es importante que" vs "Es necesario que"). The exercise tests whether the student can hear and distinguish these phrases in natural speech. Example format:
      Audio: "Es necesario que bebas mucha agua." Text: "[______] bebas mucha agua." Options: Es necesario que, Es importante que, Es mejor que.
      Audio: "Dudo que lleguen antes de las ocho." Text: "[______] lleguen antes de las ocho." Options: Dudo que, Espero que, Creo que.
      Audio: "Me alegra que puedas venir esta tarde." Text: "[______] puedas venir esta tarde." Options: Me alegra que, Me sorprende que, Quiero que.
      Audio: "Te recomiendo que pruebes el café de aquí." Text: "[______] pruebes el café de aquí." Options: Te recomiendo que, Espero que, Es mejor que.
      Audio: "Prefiero que salgamos por la mañana." Text: "[______] salgamos por la mañana." Options: Prefiero que, Quiero que, Dudo que.
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Produce the Trigger Structure">
      Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the user to translate into Spanish. The student only needs to produce the trigger phrase + que — the subjunctive verb that follows should be left as the infinitive in brackets (e.g. "Quiero que [hablar]...") since conjugating the subjunctive comes in the next lesson. The task focuses on identifying and producing the correct Spanish trigger phrase. Sentences should cover all five trigger categories across the set. Instructions visible to the student: "Translate each sentence into Spanish. Produce the trigger phrase and include the verb in infinitive form in brackets — you'll learn the full conjugation in the next lesson." Example exercises:
      1. "I hope that she can come to the party." → Espero que [poder]...
      2. "It's important that you rest today." → Es importante que [descansar]...
      3. "I recommend that you try the paella." → Te recomiendo que [probar]...
      4. "I doubt that they will arrive on time." → Dudo que [llegar]...
      5. "I'm glad that you're here." → Me alegra que [estar]...
      Generate 5 similar exercises drawn from varied trigger categories and everyday contexts.
    </unit>

  </section>

  <review>

    <unit type="word_match" name="Subjunctive or Indicative Trigger?">
      Match Spanish expressions to their grammatical effect. Explanation: "Match each expression to whether it triggers subjunctive or indicative in the following clause." Left column "Expression" contains 5 phrases — a mix of subjunctive triggers and indicative triggers (creo que, sé que, es verdad que should appear as indicative triggers alongside clear subjunctive triggers). Right column "Effect" contains only 2 options repeated as needed: Subjunctive, Indicative — with one distractor label added (e.g. "Conditional") to prevent guessing.
      Left column (5 items): Dudo que, Sé que, Es importante que, Creo que, Espero que.
      Right column (3 items): Subjunctive, Indicative, Conditional (distractor).
    </unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Indicative or Subjunctive Context — Mixed">
      Generate 6 classification exercises. The student reads a Spanish sentence and classifies whether the clause after "que" is in an indicative context or a subjunctive context. Unlike the earlier MCQ (which used English prompts), these are full Spanish sentences with the trigger phrase visible. The student must recognise the trigger phrase and classify it correctly. Same 2 options throughout: a) Indicative b) Subjunctive. The explanation: "Read each sentence and decide whether the clause after 'que' requires subjunctive or indicative." Include a mix of clear subjunctive triggers and indicative expressions (creo que, sé que, es verdad que, veo que) so the student must discriminate rather than assume subjunctive every time. Example exercises:
      — "Sé que tienes razón." → Indicative
      — "Es extraño que no haya llamado." → Subjunctive
      — "Veo que estás cansado." → Indicative
      — "Te aconsejo que llegues temprano." → Subjunctive
      — "Es verdad que el museo es increíble." → Indicative
      — "No creo que sea buena idea." → Subjunctive
      Generate 6 exercises of this type.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Trigger or Indicative — Complete the Passage">
      Generate 5 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story (a person describing a conversation with their friend about a road trip). Each sentence has one blank where either a subjunctive trigger phrase or an indicative expression (sé que, creo que, veo que) fits. The student must choose from 2-3 options. The story context determines which is correct. No hints. Example format:
      1. "____________ que el viaje va a ser perfecto." — Correct: Sé (indicative: sé que). Distractors: Dudo que, Es importante que.
      2. "Pero ____________ que practiques el español antes de ir." — Correct: Te recomiendo que. Distractors: Creo que, Sé que.
      3. "____________ que hables con los locales — es la mejor forma de aprender." — Correct: Es mejor que. Distractors: Veo que, Sé que.
      4. "____________ que puedas quedarte una semana entera." — Correct: Espero que. Distractors: Sé que, Es verdad que.
      5. "____________ que el viaje te cambie la vida." — Correct: Ojalá que. Distractors: Creo que, Es verdad que.
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="From English to Spanish Trigger Structures">
      Generate 4 exercises, each a single English sentence for the user to translate into Spanish. As in Section 2's translation unit, the student produces the trigger phrase + que, with the following verb left as an infinitive in brackets. These exercises mix all five trigger categories and include one sentence where an indicative expression is the correct choice (testing discrimination). Instructions visible to the student: "Translate each sentence. Use the correct Spanish expression and include the verb that follows in infinitive form in brackets." Example exercises:
      1. "I doubt that the train leaves on time." → Dudo que [salir]...
      2. "It's necessary that we arrive early." → Es necesario que [llegar]...
      3. "I know that the restaurant is excellent." → Sé que [ser]... (indicative — no brackets needed, full present indicative)
      4. "I'm surprised that you're here." → Me sorprende que [estar]...
      Generate 4 exercises of this difficulty and variety.
    </unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Transcribe the Trigger Sentences">
      Generate 4 isolated dictation exercises. Each sentence is a complete Spanish sentence using a subjunctive trigger phrase followed by a que clause. Sentences should be 7-10 words and draw from different trigger categories across the set. Include a brief English context line for each exercise. Sentences should use vocabulary and verb forms the student has already encountered in this lesson. Target at least one sentence with an accent mark on the trigger phrase (e.g. "ojalá", "está", "también") to test orthographic accuracy. Example exercises:
      "Espero que puedas venir a la cena." Context: "Someone inviting a friend to dinner."
      "Es importante que llegues antes de las ocho." Context: "A manager speaking to an employee."
      "Dudo que tengamos tiempo esta tarde." Context: "Someone explaining a schedule conflict."
      "Me alegra que estés bien después del viaje." Context: "A friend welcoming someone back."
    </unit>

  </review>

</lesson>
```

    - Past Tenses Mastery Review: Preterite, Imperfect, and Narrative Cohesion: - Review preterite vs. imperfect distinctions, conjugations, and usage contexts

Previous week (Week 4): Present and Future Plans: Combining Tenses

    - Discussing Plans and Intentions Across Tenses: - Combine present, near future, and future tenses in realistic conversation about life plans

    - Complete Timeline Review: Past, Present, and Future Tenses: - Review present, preterite, imperfect, near future (ir + a), and future tense in context
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises spanning all tenses learned so far
- Identify tense choices in provided passages and explain reasoning
- Production: Write a 400-word piece covering a past event, current situation, and future plans, using appropriate tenses throughout

>> CURRENT WEEK (Week 5): Introduction to Subjunctive Mood: Foundations
  Goal: Begin understanding the subjunctive mood by learning when and why it's used in Spanish. This week introduces essential contexts where subjunctive appears, preparing you to recognize and use it in practical conversational situations.

    - Present Subjunctive Irregular Forms and High-Frequency Verbs: - Cover common irregular subjunctive verbs (ser/sea, estar/esté, ir/vaya, tener/tenga, hacer/haga, poder/pueda, saber/sepa)
