<lesson>
  <context>This lesson brings together everything you have learned about the subjunctive this week — doubt and denial, impersonal expressions, and recommendations — and consolidates the key skill that runs through all of it: knowing when Spanish uses subjunctive and when it uses indicative.   <section name="Subjunctive vs. Indicative — Knowing When">

    <unit type="explanation" name="The Core Rule: When to Use Which Mood">
      Recap the fundamental distinction between subjunctive and indicative in the contexts covered this week. Frame it around a single clear principle: when the main clause expresses certainty or observable fact, the subordinate clause uses the indicative; when the main clause expresses doubt, uncertainty, emotion, impersonal judgment, or recommendation, the subordinate clause uses the subjunctive. Then cover the confusable pairs students commonly get wrong: creo que (indicative) vs. no creo que (subjunctive); es verdad que / es obvio que / es cierto que (indicative) vs. es posible que / es probable que (subjunctive); es importante que / es necesario que / es extraño que (subjunctive) vs. es evidente que / es claro que (indicative); dudo que / no es seguro que (subjunctive) vs. sé que / es seguro que (indicative). Use 2–3 side-by-side sentence pairs to show the contrast clearly. Keep examples in a Spain-move context where natural (e.g. "Sé que Madrid es una ciudad bonita." vs. "Dudo que Madrid sea barata.").
    </unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Classify the Trigger">
      Test whether students can classify a trigger phrase as requiring subjunctive or indicative. Explanation: "Decide whether the trigger phrase requires the subjunctive or the indicative in the subordinate clause." Generate 8 exercises. Each exercise presents a short Spanish sentence with the trigger phrase in bold and the subordinate verb in brackets — e.g. "**No creo que** ella [comes/coma] tarde." The options are the same for every exercise: a) Subjunctive b) Indicative. Cover all trigger types from the week: include two doubt/denial triggers (no creo que, dudo que), two impersonal subjunctive triggers (es importante que, es extraño que), one recommendation trigger (te recomiendo que), one certainty indicative trigger (es verdad que), one affirmative belief indicative trigger (creo que), and one impersonal indicative exception (es obvio que). Keep the subordinate clause content varied and plausible — daily life, plans, opinions about Spain.
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Mood — Subjunctive or Indicative">
      Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the subordinate verb blanked out. The student selects which verb form they heard from 3 options. All three options must be forms of the SAME verb but differ in mood or tense — the student must discriminate by ear. Target the near-identical sound pairs that confuse learners: subjunctive vs. present indicative (e.g. hable/habla, venga/viene, sea/es, tenga/tiene, pueda/puede). At least three exercises should feature irregular subjunctive forms. Use a variety of trigger phrases across the week's categories. For example:

      Audio: "Es importante que ella hable con su jefe antes de decidir." Text: "Es importante que ella [______] con su jefe antes de decidir." Options: hable, habla, hablará.
      Audio: "Creo que el apartamento tiene mucha luz." Text: "Creo que el apartamento [______] mucha luz." Options: tenga, tiene, tenía.
      Audio: "Dudo que el alquiler sea tan barato." Text: "Dudo que el alquiler [______] tan barato." Options: sea, es, será.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Choose the Correct Mood in Context">
      Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence with one blank — the subordinate verb — where the student must choose between a subjunctive form and an indicative form. The trigger phrase in the sentence determines the correct answer. No hints — the trigger phrase IS the context clue. Two options per blank: one subjunctive form and one indicative form of the same verb. Cover a range of trigger types: include at least one doubt trigger, one impersonal subjunctive expression, one recommendation trigger, one certainty/indicative trigger, one affirmative belief trigger, and one impersonal indicative exception. Use a Spain-planning context throughout. For example:

Lucía is encouraging and matter-of-fact; James is enthusiastic but full of doubts. The dialogue should naturally alternate between indicative sentences (where characters state facts or certainties) and subjunctive sentences (where characters express doubt, give recommendations, or make impersonal judgments). Feature at least: one creo que + indicative, one no creo que + subjunctive, one es verdad que + indicative, one es importante que + subjunctive, one te recomiendo que + subjunctive, and one es posible que + subjunctive. All woven naturally into conversation — do not make it feel like a grammar exercise. Context: they are having a café conversation about what James should expect if he moves to Madrid.
    </unit>

    <unit type="correction" name="Fix the Mood Error">

Spread error types across the five exercises: (1) indicative used after a doubt trigger — e.g. "Dudo que ella ["viene": "venga"] esta tarde." (2) subjunctive used after a certainty trigger — e.g. "Es cierto que el vuelo ["salga": "sale"] a las nueve." (3) subjunctive used after es verdad que — e.g. "Es verdad que Madrid ["tenga": "tiene"] mucho arte." (4) indicative used after an impersonal subjunctive trigger — e.g. "Es necesario que ["buscas": "busques"] un buen seguro médico." (5) indicative used after a recommendation trigger — e.g. "Te recomiendo que ["aprendes": "aprendas"] algo de español antes de llegar." Keep all sentences in a Spain-context for thematic consistency.

    <unit type="dialogue_fill" name="Advice for James — All Contexts Mixed">
      Generate a dialogue completion exercise. The user plays Elena (a Spanish friend of James who has lived in Madrid for years), and the other character is James (Británico, considering moving to Madrid). The dialogue is 8 turns total (4 each). James asks questions and expresses uncertainty; the user (Elena) responds with advice, recommendations, and impersonal judgments using subjunctive — and occasionally states certainties using indicative.     - Introduction to Subjunctive Review: Contexts and Conjugations: - Review when subjunctive is required and how to recognize trigger phrases

<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson needs to do

**Extracting learning outcomes (discarding activity suggestions):**
1. Understand the formation rule: conditional = infinitive + -ía endings (same for all regular -ar, -er, -ir verbs)
2. Conjugate regular verbs in the conditional across all 6 subject pronouns
3. Understand and use conditional for hypothetical situations ("would")
4. Understand and use conditional for polite requests

**Curriculum scope check:**
- This is Lesson 1 of Week 7 — a pure introduction to the conditional mood
- Irregular stems (tener/tendría, poder/podría etc.) belong to Lesson 2 — strictly excluded here
- Polite requests get a deep dedicated treatment in Lesson 4 — this lesson introduces the concept but doesn't need to go exhaustive
- Si-clauses are Lesson 3 — excluded
- Students know subjunctive well from Weeks 5–6, so they're comfortable with the idea that mood affects verb form for functional reasons. The conditional is a new mood, but they understand the "why" framing

**Lesson role in the week:** Foundational. Students must leave this lesson able to produce any regular conditional form and use it in both contextual registers. Everything in Lessons 2–5 builds on this formation knowledge.

---

## Step 2: Sections

Three sections:

**Section 1 — Formation of the Conditional**
Goal: Given any regular -ar, -er, or -ir verb and a subject, produce the correct conditional form.
Key insights to convey: one set of endings for ALL regular verbs; the infinitive stays intact as the stem; every form carries a written accent; the endings (-ía/-ías/-ía/-íamos/-íais/-ían) are the same regardless of verb class. This is mechanically simpler than both the indicative and subjunctive in some ways.

**Section 2 — Conditional for Hypothetical Situations**
Goal: Produce Spanish sentences expressing hypothetical "would" scenarios using the conditional correctly.
This is the primary communicative function of the conditional in Week 7. Students need to understand what "hypothetical" means contextually — not tied to si-clauses yet (that's Lesson 3), but expressing what one would do, eat, prefer, say, etc.

**Section 3 — Conditional for Polite Requests**
Goal: Produce polite conditional requests using regular verbs, and explain why the conditional is softer than present indicative.
Note: The most common polite request conditionals in Spanish use poder (¿podría/podrías?) which is irregular. Since irregular forms come in Lesson 2, this section introduces the concept using regular verb examples (¿me ayudarías?, ¿me llamarías?, ¿me explicarías?) and makes clear that the same logic applies to more common irregular forms they'll learn next. Section 4 (Lesson 4) takes polite requests much further — this is an introduction only.

---

## Step 3: Units per section

### Section 1: Formation of the Conditional
Section goal: Produce the correct conditional form for any regular verb and subject from memory.

1. **explanation** — Formation rules: infinitive intact + endings table, why all verb types share the same endings, accent on every form, quick contrast with future tense (same stem, different endings)
2. **word_match** — Match pronouns to conditional forms of a single verb (recognition of paradigm — which ending goes with which pronoun)
3. **fill_in_blanks** — 6 isolated sentences, blank the conditional verb, hint is the infinitive — mix of -ar, -er, -ir verbs; options test person confusion (e.g. hablaría vs hablarías vs hablaríamos)
4. **listening_fill** — Hear conditional sentences, select the correct person form from options (e.g. distinción between comería / comerías / comeríamos) — auditory discrimination of the accent-dependent endings
5. **write_in_blanks** — Produce conditional forms from memory, 6 isolated sentences, infinitive given as hint, no options — ramps to unscaffolded form production

### Section 2: Conditional for Hypothetical Situations
Section goal: Produce Spanish sentences expressing hypothetical scenarios using the conditional.

1. **explanation** — Hypothetical use: what would happen, what you would do/eat/prefer, expressing hypothetical preferences and desires (no si-clauses yet — standalone "would" statements)
2. **conversation** — Two characters (friends) discussing hypothetical plans: what they would do with more free time, where they would travel, what they would study — uses hablar, comer, vivir, trabajar, viajar, comprar type verbs in conditional naturally
3. **fill_in_blanks** — 6 sentences in a cohesive story expressing hypothetical scenarios, conditional verb blanked, infinitive as hint, 3 options per blank
4. **dictation** — 4 sentences expressing hypothetical conditionals from the section's vocabulary — tests auditory recognition and correct spelling of the accented endings
5. **translation** — 5 English "would" hypothetical sentences → Spanish (e.g. "I would live in Barcelona", "She would eat paella every day") — full production with no scaffolding

### Section 3: Conditional for Polite Requests
Section goal: Produce polite conditional requests using regular verbs and explain the register difference from present indicative.

1. **explanation** — Polite requests: how conditional softens a command or question (¿Me ayudas? → ¿Me ayudarías?); concept of register; note that common polite verbs like poder and querer are irregular (coming next lesson) but the same logic applies to all regular verbs
2. **conversation** — Two characters in a service/social context using conditional for polite requests with regular verbs (¿Me ayudarías?, ¿Me llamarías?, ¿Hablarías con ella?) — contrasted with blunter present forms
3. **dialogue_fill** — User completes polite request lines in a dialogue with a colleague/receptionist, conditional verbs blanked, 2 options per blank testing conditional vs present indicative
4. **translation** — 5 English polite "would" request sentences → Spanish conditional (e.g. "Would you help me with this?", "Would you call me tomorrow?") — production capstone for the section

**PARALLEL CHECK:** Sections 2 and 3 both cover contextual usage. Both follow: explanation → conversation → fill practice → translation. Section 2 uses fill_in_blanks + dictation + translation; Section 3 uses dialogue_fill + translation. The structural difference is justified: Section 2 is about declarative hypothetical statements (sentences), Section 3 is about conversational requests (dialogue format makes more sense), and Section 3 is lighter because Lesson 4 takes polite requests much further.

---

## Step 4: Review Section

No explanation. Integration goal: student discriminates between contexts (hypothetical vs. polite), produces correct regular conditional forms in both, and demonstrates fluency switching between all 6 persons.

1. **mcq** — 6 exercises classifying each sentence as "hypothetical situation" or "polite request" (same 2 options throughout) — forces conceptual discrimination
2. **fill_in_blanks** — 7 mixed sentences (both contexts), conditional verb blanked, no infinitive hints — forces contextual reading to select correct form, options include person-confusion distractors
3. **write_in_blanks** — 6 mixed sentences, blank the conditional verb, infinitive hint only — production from memory across mixed contexts
4. **dictation** — 5 mixed sentences (hypothetical + polite), tests auditory recognition and spelling accuracy across both contexts
5. **translation** — 6 English "would" sentences mixing hypothetical and polite → Spanish, no scaffolding — full production capstone

---

## Step 5: Supplementary listening additions

- **Section 1**: `listening_fill` already added (auditory discrimination of person endings — highly valuable here since the endings differ by small phonetic cues)
- **Section 2**: `dictation` already added (accent recognition in conditional forms)
- **Section 3**: No additional listening added — dialogue_fill already has audio read-along; the section is also lighter by design (Lesson 4 expands this)
- **Review**: `dictation` already added

Audio coverage: listening_fill (Section 1), dictation (Section 2), dictation (Review). Dialogue coverage: dialogue_fill (Section 3). Both structural rules satisfied.

**Dictation placement check:** Dictation appears after the student has reached production-level practice in each section — it's used to reinforce what they can already produce, not to introduce. ✓

</plan>

<lesson>
  <context>This lesson introduces the conditional tense — the Spanish equivalent of English "would." You'll learn how to form the conditional for any regular verb and explore its two core uses: expressing hypothetical situations and making polite requests.</context>

  <section name="Conditional Tense Formation">

    <unit type="explanation" name="How the Conditional Is Formed">Explain how to form the conditional tense for regular Spanish verbs. Cover: (1) the formation rule — the infinitive stays completely intact as the stem, then the endings are added directly to it; (2) the set of endings: -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían — presenting all 6 forms in a clear table; (3) the key insight that ALL regular verbs — whether -ar, -er, or -ir — use exactly the same set of endings (unlike the present indicative which differs by class); (4) the written accent on every single form without exception; (5) a brief comparison with the future tense — mention that the future uses the same infinitive stem but different endings (hablaré vs hablaría), so students should be careful not to confuse them. Give full conjugation examples for one -ar verb (hablar), one -er verb (comer), and one -ir verb (vivir), showing all 6 forms for each.</unit>

    <unit type="word_match" name="Pronouns and Conditional Endings">Match each subject pronoun to its corresponding conditional ending. Exercise explanation: "Match each subject pronoun to the correct conditional ending." Left column "Pronoun" contains 5 items: yo, tú, él/ella, nosotros, ellos/ellas. Right column "Ending" contains 8 items including distractors: -ía, -ías, -ía, -íamos, -íais, -ían, -ía (duplicate for él and yo is fine — use context), plus two non-conditional distractors such as -aba and -aré.</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Conjugating Regular Verbs in the Conditional">Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence with the conditional verb form blanked out. Include the infinitive as a hint underneath the blank. Use a mix of -ar, -er, and -ir regular verbs. 3 options per blank — the correct conditional form plus two distractors that are different person forms of the same verb (e.g. comería, comerías, comeríamos). For example:
1. "Yo [hablaría] con ella mañana." Hint: "hablar". Distractors: hablarías, hablaríamos.
2. "Nosotros [comeríamos] en ese restaurante." Hint: "comer". Distractors: comería, comerían.
3. "Ella [viviría] en el campo si pudiera." Hint: "vivir". Distractors: vivirías, viviríamos.
4. "Tú [trabajarías] muchas horas en ese trabajo." Hint: "trabajar". Distractors: trabajaría, trabajarían.
5. "Ellos [beberían] más agua cada día." Hint: "beber". Distractors: bebería, beberías.
6. "Vosotros [llevaríais] ropa cómoda." Hint: "llevar". Distractors: llevaría, llevarían.</unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Conditional — Which Person?">Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the conditional verb blanked out. The student selects which conditional form they heard from 3 options. All 3 options must be conditional forms of the SAME verb but for different subject pronouns — the student must discriminate the ending by ear. The forms should differ by suffix in ways that are auditorily subtle (e.g. -ía vs -ías vs -íamos). For example:
Audio: "Yo viviría cerca del mar." Text: "Yo [______] cerca del mar." Options: viviría, vivirías, viviríamos.
Audio: "Tú trabajarías demasiado en esa empresa." Text: "Tú [______] demasiado en esa empresa." Options: trabajarías, trabajaría, trabajaríamos.
Audio: "Ellos comerían paella todos los viernes." Text: "Ellos [______] paella todos los viernes." Options: comerían, comería, comeríais.
Audio: "Nosotros hablaríamos con el director." Text: "Nosotros [______] con el director." Options: hablaríamos, hablaría, hablarían.
Audio: "Ella compraría fruta en el mercado." Text: "Ella [______] fruta en el mercado." Options: compraría, comprarías, comprarían.</unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Produce the Conditional Form">Generate 6 isolated write-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence with the conditional verb form blanked out — the student types the answer from memory. Include the infinitive as a hint for every blank. Use a mix of -ar, -er, and -ir regular verbs and a variety of subject pronouns across the 6 exercises. For example:
1. "Yo [estudiaría] más si tuviera tiempo." Hint: "estudiar".
2. "Ellas [venderían] la casa en primavera." Hint: "vender".
3. "Tú [escribirías] un libro increíble." Hint: "escribir".
4. "Nosotros [viajaríamos] por toda España." Hint: "viajar".
5. "Él [leería] más novelas en verano." Hint: "leer".
6. "Vosotros [abriríais] una tienda en el centro." Hint: "abrir".</unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Conditional for Hypothetical Situations">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Expressing Hypothetical Situations with the Conditional">Explain that the conditional is used to talk about hypothetical situations — things that would happen under certain circumstances, or what someone would do, eat, prefer, or say in an imagined scenario. Key points: (1) the conditional in Spanish directly translates English "would + verb" in hypothetical contexts; (2) at this stage, these can be standalone statements expressing preferences or imagined scenarios (e.g. "Viviría en Barcelona" — I would live in Barcelona) — si-clauses (if I had more time, I would...) are a separate structure covered later this week; (3) the conditional is commonly used with verbs like vivir, trabajar, comer, estudiar, viajar, comprar, hablar, pasar to express hypothetical desires and scenarios; (4) give 4–5 illustrative example sentences covering different pronouns and regular verbs. Keep the focus on producing these "freestanding" hypothetical statements confidently before combining them with conditions.</unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="What Would You Do?">Generate a single conversation (8–10 lines) between two friends, Elena (28) and Marcos (30), who are chatting about what they would do if life were different. The conversation should feel natural and use the conditional mood throughout. Elena and Marcos should express hypothetical preferences about where they would live, what they would eat, what work they would do, and what they would buy — all using regular verbs in the conditional. Verbs to feature: vivir, trabajar, comer, viajar, comprar, estudiar, pasar, hablar. The tone should be relaxed and conversational. Dialogue only — no translations, annotations, or stage directions.</unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Hypothetical Scenarios — Fill the Conditional">Generate 6 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story about a person imagining their ideal life in Spain. Each sentence has one conditional verb blanked out. Include the infinitive as a hint. 3 options per blank — the correct form plus two conditional forms of the same verb for different persons. For example:
1. "En mi vida ideal, yo [viviría] en una ciudad pequeña cerca del mar." Hint: "vivir". Distractors: vivirías, vivirían.
2. "Cada mañana, [desayunaría] en una cafetería del barrio." Hint: "desayunar". Distractors: desayunarías, desayunaríamos.
3. "Mi pareja y yo [pasaríamos] los fines de semana explorando pueblos." Hint: "pasar". Distractors: pasaría, pasarías.
4. "Por las tardes, [trabajaría] desde casa sin estrés." Hint: "trabajar". Distractors: trabajarías, trabajarían.
5. "Los domingos, [comeríamos] con amigos en un restaurante tranquilo." Hint: "comer". Distractors: comería, comeríais.
6. "Sin duda, [aprendería] mucho sobre la cultura española." Hint: "aprender". Distractors: aprenderías, aprenderíamos.</unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Listen and Write — Hypothetical Conditionals">Generate 4 isolated dictation exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence using a regular verb in the conditional tense to express a hypothetical scenario. Sentences should be 7–10 words long. Include a brief English context line before each to orient the listener. Use a variety of subject pronouns and regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs. For example:
"Ella viviría en Madrid si encontrara trabajo allí." Context: "A woman describing where she would live."
"Nosotros comeríamos en ese restaurante cada semana." Context: "Two friends talking about a favourite restaurant."
"Yo estudiaría medicina si empezara de nuevo." Context: "Someone reflecting on a career choice."
"Ellos comprarían una casa en el campo." Context: "A couple discussing a hypothetical move."</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Translate Hypothetical Would-Sentences">Generate 5 translation exercises, each a single English sentence for the user to translate into Spanish using the regular conditional tense. Sentences should express hypothetical situations using a variety of regular -ar, -er, and -ir verbs and different subject pronouns. No hints or scaffolding — the student must produce the full conditional form independently. For example:
1. "I would live in Seville."
2. "She would eat seafood every day."
3. "We would work fewer hours."
4. "They would travel around Spain every summer."
5. "You (tú) would write a lot of emails in that job."</unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Conditional for Polite Requests">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Making Polite Requests with the Conditional">Explain that the conditional is also used to make requests and questions more polite and less direct. Key points: (1) the present indicative can sound blunt in requests (¿Me ayudas? — Will you help me?), while the conditional softens it (¿Me ayudarías? — Would you help me?); (2) this works with any verb — e.g. ¿Me llamarías? (Would you call me?), ¿Me explicarías esto? (Would you explain this to me?); (3) the most commonly heard polite conditionals in Spanish are ¿Podría...? and ¿Querría...? — mention that poder and querer are irregular and will be covered next lesson, but the same logic applies to all regular verbs; (4) provide 4–5 side-by-side examples contrasting present indicative (blunt) vs conditional (polite) using only regular verbs; (5) note that the conditional of politeness is especially common in service situations, professional contexts, and when asking favours of people you don't know well.</unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Polite Requests in Context">Generate a single conversation (8–10 lines) between two characters: Sara (32), a new employee, and Rafael (45), a colleague she doesn't know well yet. Sara is asking for various favours at work. The conversation should contrast a mix of polite conditional forms (¿Me ayudarías?, ¿Me explicarías...?, ¿Me enviarías...?) with Rafael's friendly responses. Use only regular verbs in the conditional for Sara's requests. The dialogue should feel realistic and warm — Rafael is happy to help. Dialogue only — no translations, annotations, or stage directions.</unit>

    <unit type="dialogue_fill" name="Complete the Polite Requests">Generate a dialogue completion exercise. The scenario: the user (Tú) is asking a hotel receptionist for several favours. The receptionist (Recepcionista) is helpful and professional. The dialogue is 8 turns total (4 each). The user's lines contain blanks for the conditional verb forms. 2 options per blank — the correct conditional form and a present indicative form of the same verb (testing whether the student chooses the polite register). No hints — the conversational context and the instruction to be polite make the answer clear. For example:

Recepcionista: Buenos días, ¿en qué le puedo ayudar?

Recepcionista: Claro, es muy sencillo. Siga todo recto y gire a la derecha.
Tú: Perfecto. ¿[me llamaría] un taxi para las diez, por favor? Distractors: me llama.
Recepcionista: Enseguida lo hago.
Tú: Muchas gracias. ¿[me dejaría] la llave de la habitación también? Distractors: me deja.</unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Translate Polite Request Sentences">Generate 5 translation exercises, each a single English polite request sentence for the user to translate into Spanish using the regular conditional tense. Use a variety of regular verbs and both ¿...? question form and statement form. No hints. For example:
1. "Would you help me with this form?"
2. "Would you call me tomorrow morning?"
3. "Would you explain this to me, please?"
4. "Would you send me the document?"
5. "Would you open the window?"</unit>

  </section>

output:
<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to teach

**Learning outcomes (stripping away activity format suggestions):**
1. Recognise the irregular conditional stems and understand they follow consistent patterns
2. Conjugate the high-frequency irregular conditional verbs (tener, venir, salir, poner, poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer) across all persons
3. Use irregular conditional forms in polite request and hypothetical sentence contexts
4. Produce original sentences with irregular conditional verbs expressing hypothetical situations

**Key curriculum considerations:**
- Lesson 1 this week already covered the -ía ending system and the conceptual use of conditional for hypotheticals and polite requests. This lesson does NOT need to re-explain those concepts — it builds squarely on them. The job here is learning the irregular stems.
- Lesson 3 covers si-clauses; Lesson 4 covers polite requests and speculation in depth. This lesson introduces irregular stems and begins using them in context, but the nuanced polite/speculative usage doesn't need to be pushed hard here.
- The curriculum note says "same as future tense irregulars" — but future tense hasn't been explicitly taught in this curriculum. The pedagogical shortcut is useful if introduced here ("these same stems will reappear"), but the stems must be taught from scratch.
- This is a pattern-learning lesson. There are ~9 high-frequency irregular verbs to cover. The ramp needs to be verb-by-verb recognition first, then production across all persons, then contextual use.

---

## Step 2: Breaking into sections

There are too many irregular verbs to dump in one section. They split naturally into two pattern groups:

**Group 1 — D-insertion**: The infinitive vowel is dropped and -d- is inserted before the endings.
- tener → tendr-, venir → vendr-, salir → saldr-, poner → pondr-
- Four core high-frequency verbs; clean pattern; a strong anchor for the lesson

**Group 2 — Vowel drop + unique stems**: Either the infinitive vowel is dropped (without inserting -d-) or the stem changes more dramatically.
- poder → podr-, saber → sabr- (vowel drop)
- hacer → har-, decir → dir-, querer → querr- (unique)
- Five verbs; two sub-patterns that can be explained together as "the rest"

This split gives each section a clear pattern to anchor on. It mirrors how textbooks group them and how learners mentally file them.

**No third section on polite requests/hypotheticals**: The student already understands conditional use from Lesson 1. Polite/hypothetical usage is the vehicle for contextual practice in this lesson, not new teaching material — it belongs in the review and in section exercises, not its own section.

**Sections:**
1. D-insertion stems (tener, venir, salir, poner)
2. Vowel-drop and unique stems (poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer)
3. Review (integration across all 9 verbs, production in realistic contexts)

---

## Step 3: Units per section (core only first)

### Section 1: D-Insertion Stems

Section goal: Given any of tener, venir, salir, poner and any subject pronoun, the student can produce the correct conditional form from memory.

- **explanation**: Introduce the d-insertion pattern — these four verbs drop their infinitive vowel and insert -d-, then take standard -ía endings. Show full conjugation of tener as the model, then give the stems for venir, salir, poner. Mention this same pattern applies to valer and a few others. Note the connection to future tense irregulars (the student will see these again).
- **flashcard**: The four key yo-forms as translation anchors — tendría/I would have, vendría/I would come, saldría/I would leave, pondría/I would put. Double-sided.
- **word_match**: Match infinitive (left) to its conditional yo-form (right) — tener, venir, salir, poner on the left; tendría, vendría, saldría, pondría plus 2 regular verb distractors on the right.
- **fill_in_blanks**: 5 isolated sentences with these verbs blanked out across various subjects (not just yo). Hint = infinitive. 3 distractors per blank drawn from other persons of the same verb.
- **write_in_blanks**: 4 isolated sentences with the conditional verb blanked. No options — student types. Hint = infinitive + subject pronoun where needed (when subject pronoun is absent from sentence). These sentences use all four verbs in polite/hypothetical contexts.
- **conversation**: Short 2-character scripted dialogue (8–10 lines) featuring tener, venir, salir, poner in conditional — e.g., two friends discussing hypothetical plans and politely suggesting things.

PARALLEL NOTE: Section 2 will mirror this exact structure.

### Section 2: Vowel-Drop and Unique Stems

Section goal: Given any of poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer and any subject pronoun, the student can produce the correct conditional form from memory.

- **explanation**: Introduce the two remaining sub-patterns. First: vowel drop (poder→podr-, saber→sabr-) — the infinitive ending is simply trimmed. Second: fully unique stems (hacer→har-, decir→dir-, querer→querr-) — these must be memorised, no shortcut. Show full paradigm of hacer as the model for unique stems. Note that podría and sabría are high-frequency for polite requests specifically.
- **flashcard**: yo-forms as anchors — podría/I would be able to, sabría/I would know, haría/I would do, diría/I would say, querría/I would want. Double-sided.
- **word_match**: Match infinitive to conditional yo-form — poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer on left; podría, sabría, haría, diría, querría plus 2 d-insertion distractors (tendría, saldría) on right.
- **fill_in_blanks**: 5 isolated sentences with these verbs blanked across various subjects. Hint = infinitive. Distractors drawn from other persons of same verb plus one d-insertion form to keep the groups distinct.
- **write_in_blanks**: 4 isolated sentences with conditional verb blanked. Student types. Hint = infinitive + subject pronoun where needed. Sentences use polite requests and hypothetical situations.
- **conversation**: Short 2-character dialogue (8–10 lines) featuring poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer in conditional — e.g., one character asking politely for help, the other speculating about what they could do.

PARALLEL TO Section 1 — same structure, confirmed.

### Review

Review goal: Integrate all 9 irregular conditional verbs across mixed subjects, polite requests, and hypothetical scenarios at production level.

No explanation — the student has full confidence in each group individually; the review tests discrimination and switching.

- **mcq**: "What is the correct conditional yo form of X?" — 8 questions covering all 9 verbs (some appear in non-yo forms), with 3 distractors each drawn from: the wrong irregular pattern, the regular conditional, and another irregular verb's form. This is identification, not sentence-fill — valid MCQ.
- **fill_in_blanks (cohesive story)**: A continuous 6-sentence story mixing all 9 irregular verbs across different subjects. One blank per sentence. No hints. Distractors drawn from both groups plus regular verbs. The student must discriminate which stem group each verb belongs to.
- **correction**: 6 isolated sentences where an incorrect stem is used (e.g., *hacería* instead of *haría*, *ponería* instead of *pondría*). Each sentence has 1 error highlighted. The student must rewrite the correct form.
- **translation**: 5 English sentences using "would" with irregular conditional verbs in polite request and hypothetical contexts — student translates into Spanish. Mix of subjects and verbs.
- **writing_practice**: Open-ended prompt — imagine your life if you moved to Spain next year. What would you do, say, know, be able to do? Student must use at least 4 different irregular conditional verbs from the lesson. Up to ~6–8 sentences.

---

## Step 4: Supplementary listening units

**Section 1**: The d-insertion forms sound distinct from regular conditionals and from the yo present form — "tendría" vs "tiene" vs "tenía." Add **listening_fill** after fill_in_blanks — hear the verb in context and select which d-insertion form was used. Options are same verb in different persons or a regular conditional form as distractor.

**Section 2**: "Podría" and "haría" are extremely high-frequency in polite speech and sound very different from their infinitives. These forms are worth hearing. Add **listening_fill** after fill_in_blanks — same approach.

**Review**: By the review, the student has reached production. Add **dictation** (4 sentences) at the end of the review — short sentences using irregular conditionals in realistic contexts. This tests whether they can hear and accurately transcribe the -ía endings on these stems. No context hints (student is at B1 level and the sentences are short). Placed after translation, before writing_practice.

No listening_word_order — the word order of conditional sentences in Spanish is not markedly different from what the student knows, and the lesson's challenge is stem recall, not sentence structure. Adding word order exercises would dilute focus without proportionate benefit.

---

## Final structure:

**Section 1** (7 units): explanation → flashcard → word_match → fill_in_blanks → listening_fill → write_in_blanks → conversation

**Section 2** (7 units): explanation → flashcard → word_match → fill_in_blanks → listening_fill → write_in_blanks → conversation

**Review** (6 units): mcq → fill_in_blanks → correction → translation → dictation → writing_practice

<context>This lesson covers the irregular conditional verbs — the most important "would" forms in Spanish that don't follow the standard infinitive-plus-endings pattern. <section name="D-Insertion Stems: tener, venir, salir, poner">

Note that these same stems appear in the future tense — the student will see them again. Give 2–3 example sentences in realistic contexts: "¿Tendría usted un momento?" / "Vendría contigo, pero estoy ocupado." / "¿A qué hora saldrías?"</unit>

<unit type="flashcard" name="D-Insertion Yo-Forms">Generate a double-sided flashcard deck of 4 cards. Each card has the Spanish yo-form conditional on one side and the English translation on the other. Cards:
tendría -> I would have
vendría -> I would come
saldría -> I would leave / go out
pondría -> I would put / place</unit>

<unit type="word_match" name="D-Insertion Stems Match">Match each infinitive to its correct conditional yo-form. Explanation: "Match each infinitive verb to its correct conditional yo-form." Each exercise is a Spanish sentence with the conditional verb blanked out. The student types the correct form — no options provided. Since subject pronouns are sometimes omitted, include the subject + infinitive as a hint for every blank. Sentences should use polite requests and hypothetical contexts. For example:

<unit type="conversation" name="Making Hypothetical Plans">Generate a conversation between two characters, Lucía (28, enthusiastic, planning a trip) and David (30, more cautious, pragmatic). The conversation is set in a café in Madrid where they're discussing a hypothetical road trip across Spain. The dialogue should be 10 lines (5 each) and feature the conditional forms vendría, tendría, saldría, and pondría (any persons) used naturally. The characters should express what they would do, what they would bring, when they would leave, and any hesitations. The tone is warm and natural — two friends genuinely planning something exciting.</unit>

</section>

<section name="Vowel-Drop and Unique Stems: poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer">

Include subject + infinitive as a hint for every blank. Sentences should use polite requests and hypothetical scenarios — reflect real situations a Spanish learner might encounter. For example:
1. "¿[Podría] usted repetir eso más despacio?" Hint: "usted - poder".
2. "Yo no [sabría] qué hacer en esa situación." Hint: "yo - saber".
3. "[Diría] que es mejor esperar." Hint: "yo - decir".
4. "¿[Harías] lo mismo otra vez?" Hint: "tú - hacer".</unit>

<unit type="conversation" name="Asking for Help and Speculating">Generate a conversation between two characters, Ana (35, new to a neighbourhood, slightly lost) and Jorge (40, friendly local, helpful). Ana is trying to find a post office and asks for directions and help politely. The dialogue should be 10 lines (5 each) and feature the conditional forms podría, sabría, haría, diría, and querría (any persons) used naturally in polite requests and helpful suggestions. The register is warm but slightly formal (strangers on the street) — Ana uses polite conditional forms when asking, Jorge uses conditional when suggesting what he would do or saying what he knows. End with a natural conversational close.</unit>

</section>

<review>

<unit type="mcq" name="Identify the Correct Irregular Conditional Form">Test knowledge of all nine irregular conditional stems. Explanation: "Choose the correct conditional form of each verb." Generate 9 exercises — one for each irregular verb (tener, venir, salir, poner, poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer). Each exercise presents the infinitive and a subject pronoun, and the student selects the correct conditional form from 4 options. Distractors for each item should include: the regular conditional (if it were regular, e.g., *hacería* for hacer), a form from the wrong irregular group (e.g., *pondría* as a distractor for poder), and a correct form of a different person. For example:

"hacer — yo"
a) hacería
b) haría
c) hicería
d) pondría

"tener — ellos"
a) tenerían
b) tienerían
c) tendrían
d) podrían</unit>

<unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="All Irregular Conditionals in Context">Generate 6 fill-in-the-blank exercises as a cohesive story. A Spanish person is imagining what their life would be like if they won the lottery. Each sentence has one blank — a conditional verb from the full set of nine irregular verbs covered in this lesson. Vary subjects across the passage (yo, mi familia, mis amigos, nosotros, etc.). No hints. Distractors per blank: one regular conditional form of the same verb, one form of a different irregular verb, one form of the same verb in a different person. The story should read naturally and coherently when completed. Example sentences to guide tone:
"Lo primero que [haría] sería comprar una casa en el sur de España."
"Mis padres no [tendrían] que trabajar más."
"[Podría] viajar a todos los lugares que siempre he soñado conocer."</unit>

<unit type="correction" name="Fix the Irregular Conditional Stem">Generate 6 isolated correction exercises. Each sentence contains one highlighted error: a conditional verb where a wrong stem has been used — typically a regularised form (e.g., *hacería* instead of *haría*), a cross-group confusion (e.g., *podría* used for *pondría* or vice versa), or wrong person on an irregular stem. The correct form must be a single, unambiguous fix. Draw errors from across both stem groups. For example:
"¿["harías": "hacerías"] algo diferente si pudieras?" (regularised stem)
"No ["sabría": "sabrería"] cómo explicarlo." (regularised vowel-drop)
"¿["Pondría": "Podría"] usted repetir la pregunta?" (d-insertion/vowel-drop confusion — wrong direction)
"Ellos ["vendrían": "venirían"] si los invitáramos." (regularised stem)</unit>

<unit type="translation" name="Translate Irregular Conditional Sentences">Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the student to translate into Spanish. All sentences use "would" with one or more of the nine irregular conditional verbs from this lesson. Cover a mix of polite requests and hypothetical situations. Vary subjects and avoid repeating the same verb twice. For example:
1. "What would you do in that situation?" (hacer — tú)
2. "Could you tell me where the station is?" (poder — usted, polite request)
3. "She would come, but she has to work." (venir)
4. "We would know if we had asked." (saber — nosotros)
5. "He said he would put the keys on the table." (poner — él)</unit>

<unit type="dictation" name="Irregular Conditional Dictation">Generate 4 isolated dictation exercises. Each sentence uses at least one irregular conditional verb from this lesson and is 6–9 words long. No context hints — the student listens and transcribes the full sentence including correct spelling and accent marks. Sentences should reflect real conversational contexts — polite requests, hypothetical situations, and speculation. Cover different verbs and persons across the four sentences. For example:
"¿Podrías ayudarme con esto, por favor?"
"Yo no sabría qué hacer en esa situación."
"¿Qué harían ellos si no tuvieran dinero?"
"Dijo que vendría a las ocho de la noche."</unit>

<unit type="writing_practice" name="What Would You Do?">Generate a writing prompt asking the student to imagine they have one free week in Spain — no plans, no commitments, no budget worries. What would they do? What would they say to people? What would they want to see or experience? Objectives:
1. Use at least five different irregular conditional verbs from this lesson (tener, venir, salir, poner, poder, saber, hacer, decir, querer).
2. Include at least one polite request using conditional (e.g., asking a local for a recommendation).
3. Write 6–10 sentences using a variety of subjects (yo, nosotros, and at least one third person).</unit>

</review>

</lesson>

- Practice through flashcard-style conjugation exercises
- Translate 10 English sentences containing subjunctive-triggering expressions into Spanish

Previous week (Week 6): Subjunctive in Doubt, Negation, and Impersonal Expressions

- Fill-in-the-blank exercises with impersonal expression triggers
- Write original sentences using impersonal expressions with subjunctive to give advice and express necessity
    - Subjunctive in Recommendations and Advice: - Practice subjunctive after recommendation verbs and impersonal advice phrases
- Understand how subjunctive naturally conveys recommendations (te recomiendo que, te sugiero que, deberías)
- Complete dialogue-based exercises where characters give advice using subjunctive
- Write conversational exchanges where you recommend actions to others using subjunctive
    - Subjunctive in Everyday Conversational Contexts: - Apply subjunctive to realistic social situations: expressing opinions, doubts, desires about daily life
- Practice with scenario-based exercises (workplace, relationships, travel planning)

>> CURRENT WEEK (Week 7): Conditional Mood and Mixed Tenses
  Goal: Master the conditional mood to express hypothetical situations, polite requests, and speculations. This week teaches you to construct complex sentences combining multiple tenses and moods for sophisticated communication.

    - Si (If) Clauses: Real and Hypothetical Conditions: - Learn real conditional structure: present indicative + future/ir + a (Si estudias, aprenderás)

  Goal: Master sophisticated connectors and discourse markers that enable natural, flowing conversation. This week bridges all grammar learned so far, teaching you to structure longer passages and discussions with professional-level Spanish cohesion.

output:
<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to teach

**Extracting learning outcomes (discarding activity format suggestions):**
1. Understand and produce **real conditional si-clauses**: si + present indicative → future tense (or ir + a + infinitive)
2. Understand and form the **imperfect subjunctive** — a new conjugation the student hasn't encountered yet, required for hypothetical si-clauses
3. Understand and produce **hypothetical conditional si-clauses**: si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional
4. Discriminate between real and hypothetical si-clauses and choose the correct structure based on context

**Critical gap identified from curriculum:** The student has learned *present* subjunctive in weeks 5-6 and the conditional tense in lessons 1-2 this week. But the **imperfect subjunctive** (-ara/-iera endings) has not been explicitly introduced anywhere in the curriculum prior to this lesson. It is essential for hypothetical si-clauses and must be taught here before using it in the full structure. This is the single biggest teaching burden of the lesson.

**The student already brings:**
- Conditional tense (regular and irregular) — lessons 1 & 2 this week
- Present subjunctive (regular and irregular) — weeks 5 & 6
- Present indicative — established early in curriculum
- Near future: ir + a + infinitive — assumed as baseline knowledge

## Step 2: Splitting into sections

**Three learning sections make sense here:**

**Section 1 — Real Si-Clauses**: si + present indicative → future/ir + a. Simpler structure, uses familiar tenses, low barrier. Establishes si-clause word order logic before the harder hypothetical structure.

**Section 2 — Imperfect Subjunctive Formation**: A brand-new conjugation system. Derive from ellos preterite → drop -on → add -ra endings. Cover regular -ar and -er/-ir patterns, plus high-frequency irregulars (fuera, tuviera, pudiera, hiciera, dijera, etc.). Must be its own section because the student cannot use hypothetical si-clauses without first being able to produce this form reliably. This section isolates the conjugation problem before applying it in Section 3.

**Section 3 — Hypothetical Si-Clauses**: si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional. Combines Section 2's new conjugation with the conditional from lessons 1 & 2. This section should ramp fully to production since the student has all the ingredients by now.

**Why not merge Sections 2 and 3?** The imperfect subjunctive is genuinely new material — it has different derivation logic from the present subjunctive, and the student needs to practice forming it before being asked to embed it inside a si-clause structure. Merging would create an overloaded section that teaches a conjugation system and applies it in a complex clause structure simultaneously.

**Parallel structure check:** Sections 1 and 3 are parallel — both teach a si-clause type. They should share the same ramp structure: explanation → conversation → fill_in_blanks → write_in_blanks → translation. I'll confirm this when designing units. Section 2 is conjugation-focused and differs structurally (no conversation, more form-drilling).

## Step 3: Units per section

**Section 1 — Real Si-Clauses**
*Goal: Student can produce real conditional si-clauses using present indicative + future/ir + a.*

1. **Explanation** — Real si-clauses: structure, word order (either clause can come first), use of future vs. ir + a. Show several examples with varied verbs.
2. **Conversation** — Two characters making realistic plans contingent on conditions (weather, study results, timing). Shows real si-clauses naturally in speech.
3. **Fill in blanks** — Sentences with either the si-clause verb or the result clause verb blanked. Options include present indicative, future, and distractors (conditional — wrong for real si-clauses).
4. **Word order** — Unscramble real si-clause sentences (some with si-clause first, some with main clause first).
5. **Translation** (EN→ES) — 5 English "if" sentences that clearly imply real/plausible conditions → student produces full si-clause.

*Listening addition:*
6. **Listening fill** — Hear real si-clause sentences; identify whether the blanked verb is in the present indicative (si-clause) or the future (result clause). Distractors include conditional forms to reinforce that conditional does NOT follow si in real clauses.

**Section 2 — Imperfect Subjunctive Formation**
*Goal: Student can form the imperfect subjunctive for regular verbs and key irregular verbs.*

1. **Explanation** — Derivation from ellos preterite: hablar → hablaron → hablara/hablaras/hablara/habláramos/hablarais/hablaran. Same logic for -er/-ir: comer → comieran; vivir → vivieran. Key irregulars: ser/ir → fuera, tener → tuviera, poder → pudiera, hacer → hiciera, decir → dijera, saber → supiera.
2. **Word match** — Match infinitives to their imperfect subjunctive yo-form. Left column: 5 infinitives (hablar, ser, tener, hacer, venir). Right column: 10 imperfect subjunctive forms including distractors.
3. **Fill in blanks** — Isolated sentences with the imperfect subjunctive verb blanked. Infinitive hint given under each blank. Options include ellos preterite form and present subjunctive as distractors (to distinguish from forms student already knows).
4. **Write in blanks** — Isolated sentences with the imperfect subjunctive verb blanked and only the infinitive as hint. Student types the full imperfect subjunctive form.
5. **Dictation** — Hear short sentences containing imperfect subjunctive forms, transcribe them. Tests whether student can hear and write the -ara/-iera endings accurately.

**Section 3 — Hypothetical Si-Clauses**
*Goal: Student can produce hypothetical conditional si-clauses using si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional.*

*PARALLEL TO Section 1*

1. **Explanation** — Hypothetical si-clauses: si + imperfect subjunctive in the si-clause, conditional in the result clause. Contrast clearly with real si-clauses (the structural difference signals likelihood/reality). Note that conditional NEVER follows si directly.
2. **Conversation** — Two characters discussing hypothetical scenarios (what they would do if they won money, if they lived in Spain, if they had time). Shows hypothetical si-clauses naturally in dialogue.
3. **Fill in blanks** — Hypothetical si-clause sentences with either clause verb blanked. Options include imperfect subjunctive, conditional, and distractors (present indicative, future — wrong for hypothetical si-clauses).
4. **Write in blanks** — Hypothetical si-clause sentences with both clause verbs blanked. Infinitive hint for each. Student must produce the imperfect subjunctive AND the conditional from scratch.
5. **Translation** (EN→ES) — 5 English hypothetical "if" sentences (clearly counterfactual/unlikely) → student produces full hypothetical si-clause.

*Listening addition:*
6. **Listening fill** — Hear hypothetical si-clause sentences; identify the blanked imperfect subjunctive form. Distractors are present subjunctive and present indicative forms that sound similar but differ in ending.

## Step 4: Review

The review mixes real and hypothetical si-clauses. The student has been fully confident in each type separately — now they must discriminate in real time.

1. **MCQ** — Classify sentences as real conditional or hypothetical conditional. 8 sentences, same two options throughout. Tests conceptual discrimination.
2. **Fill in blanks** — Mixed si-clause sentences (some real, some hypothetical), one blank per sentence. Student must read context, determine the si-clause type, and select the correct verb form. Distractors span all four tenses/moods (present indicative, future, imperfect subjunctive, conditional).
3. **Correction** — Short passage with highlighted errors: real si-clause structure used where hypothetical is needed, conditional incorrectly placed after si, imperfect subjunctive used where present indicative belongs. Student rewrites the highlighted wrong forms.
4. **Translation** (EN→ES) — 5 mixed English "if" sentences (some clearly real, some clearly hypothetical). Student must determine the type and produce the full structure.
5. **Writing practice** — Open-ended: student writes about what they would do if they moved to Spain (hypothetical) and what they will do if their Spanish improves (real). Requires both si-clause types.

*Listening addition:*
6. **Dictation** — Hear 4 mixed si-clause sentences (real and hypothetical). Tests whether student can hear the structural difference — imperfect subjunctive vs. present indicative in the si-clause — and transcribe it accurately.

## Step 5: Supplementary units audit
- Section 1: listening_fill added ✓ (hear future vs. conditional — key confusion)
- Section 2: dictation added ✓ (new conjugation needs aural reinforcement)
- Section 3: listening_fill added ✓ (hear imperfect subjunctive vs. similar forms)
- Review: dictation added ✓ (integration — hear real vs. hypothetical in mixed set)

Every section has at least one listening unit. Every section has at least one dialogue or listening unit. ✓

**Parallel structure confirmed:**
- Section 1: explanation → conversation → fill_in_blanks → word_order → translation → listening_fill
- Section 3: explanation → conversation → fill_in_blanks → write_in_blanks → translation → listening_fill
- Difference: Section 1 has word_order (unscramble, lighter production — appropriate since it's the first si-clause section and imperfect subjunctive isn't in play yet). Section 3 has write_in_blanks (free recall — appropriate since the student has both clause components and needs to produce both from scratch). This content-level difference justifies the structural divergence.

</plan>
