<lesson>
  <context>This lesson teaches you how to build "if" sentences (si-clauses) in Spanish — both the real kind ("If I study, I will pass") and the hypothetical kind ("If I studied, I would pass"). You'll also learn the imperfect subjunctive, a new verb form that powers hypothetical si-clauses, and by the end you'll be able to switch between both structures confidently.</context>

  <section name="Real Si-Clauses: Present + Future">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Real Si-Clauses: Structure and Logic">
      Explain real conditional si-clauses in Spanish. Cover the following:

      - The structure: si + present indicative → future tense (or ir + a + infinitive in the result clause)
      - Both clauses can be ordered either way: "Si estudias, aprobarás" and "Aprobarás si estudias" are both correct
      - Real si-clauses describe plausible, realistic conditions — things that could genuinely happen
      - A critical rule: the conditional tense NEVER directly follows si. Only present indicative goes in the si-clause for real conditions
      - Show at least 6 varied examples using different verbs and both word orders (si-clause first and result clause first)
      - Use examples that feel natural and relevant: studying, travel plans, weather conditions, work scenarios
      - Briefly note that there is a second type of si-clause (hypothetical) that will be covered later in the lesson, to prepare the student for what's coming
    </unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Making Plans with Real Si-Clauses">
      Generate a single conversation (8-10 lines) between two characters — Elena (28, a teacher preparing for a trip) and Marcos (30, her colleague). They are discussing realistic plans that depend on conditions: whether Elena finishes her marking, whether the train tickets are affordable, whether the weather is good. The dialogue should contain at least 5 real si-clauses using a variety of verbs (estudiar, terminar, llover, poder, querer, ir). The conversation should feel natural and casual — two friends making tentative plans. Use only the si + present indicative → future structure throughout.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Complete the Real Si-Clause">
      Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a complete Spanish si-clause sentence with ONE verb blanked — sometimes the verb in the si-clause (present indicative), sometimes the verb in the result clause (future tense). Include the infinitive as a hint under each blank. 3 options per blank: the correct form, plus two distractors drawn from: conditional tense of the same verb (the most important distractor — reinforcing that conditional does NOT follow si), imperfect subjunctive of the same verb, or a different person/number of the correct tense.

      Example exercises:
      1. "Si [hablas] con el director, él te ayudará." Hint: "hablar". Distractors: hablarías, hablaras.
      2. "Llegaremos a tiempo si el tren no [llega] tarde." Hint: "llegar". Distractors: llegaría, llegara.
      3. "Si tienes hambre, [comeremos] algo antes de salir." Hint: "comer". Distractors: comeríamos, comiéramos.
      4. "Si el apartamento es barato, lo [alquilaré]." Hint: "alquilar". Distractors: alquilaría, alquilara.
      5. "Te [llamaré] si necesito ayuda." Hint: "llamar". Distractors: llamaría, llamara.
      6. "Si [estudias] todos los días, aprenderás rápido." Hint: "estudiar". Distractors: estudiarías, estudiaras.
    </unit>

    <unit type="word_order" name="Build the Real Si-Clause">
      Generate 6 word order exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish real conditional si-clause sentence to unscramble. Include the English translation for each so the student knows what they're building. Sentences should be 7-11 words. Mix the order: some sentences with the si-clause first, some with the result clause first. Use varied verbs and topics.

      Example sentences:
      1. "Si comes bien, tendrás más energía." ("If you eat well, you will have more energy.")
      2. "Visitaremos el museo si hay tiempo." ("We will visit the museum if there is time.")
      3. "Si no llueve mañana, iremos a la playa." ("If it doesn't rain tomorrow, we will go to the beach.")
      4. "Hablaré con ella si me llama esta tarde." ("I will talk to her if she calls me this afternoon.")
      5. "Si buscas trabajo, encontrarás algo mejor." ("If you look for work, you will find something better.")
      6. "Podremos salir si terminamos antes de las seis." ("We will be able to leave if we finish before six.")
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Translate Real Si-Clauses">
      Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the student to translate into Spanish. All sentences use real conditional si-clauses — plausible, realistic conditions expressed with si + present indicative + future. Vary the subjects, verbs, and whether the si-clause comes first or second in the sentence.

      Example sentences:
      1. "If you call him, he will answer."
      2. "We will go to the park if the weather is nice."
      3. "If she studies every day, she will pass the exam."
      4. "I will help you if you need it."
      5. "If you are hungry, we will eat something."
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Real Si-Clause Forms">
      Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is a real conditional si-clause spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with one verb blanked — always the verb in the result clause (future tense). The student must listen and identify whether they heard the future tense or the conditional tense, since these sound similar but only the future is correct in real si-clauses. 3 options per blank: the correct future tense form, the conditional form of the same verb, and the present indicative form of the same verb.

      Example exercises:
      Audio: "Si estudias esta semana, aprobarás el examen."
      Text: "Si estudias esta semana, [______] el examen."
      Options: aprobarás, aprobarías, apruebas.

      Audio: "Si terminas pronto, podremos salir juntos."
      Text: "Si terminas pronto, [______] salir juntos."
      Options: podremos, podríamos, podemos.

      Audio: "Si hace buen tiempo mañana, iremos a la playa."
      Text: "Si hace buen tiempo mañana, [______] a la playa."
      Options: iremos, iríamos, vamos.
    </unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Imperfect Subjunctive Formation">

    <unit type="explanation" name="The Imperfect Subjunctive: How to Form It">
      Explain the imperfect subjunctive in Spanish. Cover the following clearly and methodically:

      **Derivation rule:**
      - Start from the ellos/ellas form of the preterite tense
      - Drop the -on ending completely
      - Add the imperfect subjunctive endings

      **Endings for -ar verbs** (from hablaron → hablar-):
      - hablara, hablaras, hablara, habláramos, hablarais, hablaran

      **Endings for -er and -ir verbs** (from comieron → comier-, vivieran → vivier-):
      - comiera, comieras, comiera, comiéramos, comierais, comieran
      - viviera, vivieras, viviera, viviéramos, vivierais, vivieran

      **Why this derivation matters:** Because any irregularity in the ellos preterite carries directly into the imperfect subjunctive — you only need to learn one irregular stem.

      **Key irregular forms to cover explicitly** (giving ellos preterite → imperfect subjunctive yo form):
      - ser/ir: fueran → fuera
      - tener: tuvieran → tuviera
      - poder: pudieran → pudiera
      - hacer: hicieran → hiciera
      - decir: dijeran → dijera
      - saber: supieran → supiera
      - estar: estuvieran → estuviera
      - venir: vinieran → viniera

      Show the full conjugation table for at least one regular -ar verb and one irregular verb. Note that the imperfect subjunctive will be used immediately in hypothetical si-clauses in the next section.
    </unit>

    <unit type="word_match" name="Infinitive to Imperfect Subjunctive">
      Match infinitives to their imperfect subjunctive yo form. This is a quick-recall check of the forms introduced in the explanation.

      Explanation: "Match each infinitive to the correct imperfect subjunctive yo form."
      Left column "Infinitive" (5 items): hablar, ser, tener, poder, hacer.
      Right column "Imperfect Subj." (10 items including distractors): hablara, fuera, tuviera, pudiera, hiciera, hablase, fuese, tenga, pueda, haga.

      The 5 correct pairs are: hablar → hablara, ser → fuera, tener → tuviera, poder → pudiera, hacer → hiciera. The 5 distractors (hablase, fuese, tenga, pueda, haga) are plausible-looking but wrong — either present subjunctive forms or -se alternative subjunctive endings.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Recognise the Imperfect Subjunctive">
      Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence with the imperfect subjunctive verb blanked out. Include the infinitive as a hint under each blank. 3 options per blank: the correct imperfect subjunctive form, the present subjunctive form of the same verb (a key distractor — looks similar but different ending), and the ellos preterite form (the form students derive from — reinforcing that they must change the ending).

      Example exercises:
      1. "Quería que tú [hablaras] con el director." Hint: "hablar". Distractors: hables, hablaron.
      2. "Era importante que ella [tuviera] más tiempo." Hint: "tener". Distractors: tenga, tuvieron.
      3. "Él esperaba que nosotros [pudiéramos] ayudar." Hint: "poder". Distractors: podamos, pudieron.
      4. "Me pidió que yo [fuera] al mercado." Hint: "ir". Distractors: vaya, fueron.
      5. "Dudaban que él [hiciera] el trabajo solo." Hint: "hacer". Distractors: haga, hicieron.
      6. "Necesitaba que vosotros [vinierais] temprano." Hint: "venir". Distractors: vengáis, vinieron.
    </unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Produce the Imperfect Subjunctive">
      Generate 6 isolated write-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a Spanish sentence with the imperfect subjunctive verb blanked out. The student must type the correct imperfect subjunctive form from memory — no options given. Include the infinitive as a hint for every blank, and where there is no explicit subject pronoun in the sentence, also include the subject as part of the hint (joined with a dash).

      Example exercises:
      1. "Ella quería que yo [hablara] más despacio." Hint: "hablar".
      2. "[Pudieras] venir mañana sería perfecto." Hint: "tú - poder".
      3. "Era necesario que el equipo [tuviera] más práctica." Hint: "tener".
      4. "Nos sorprendió que [fuera] tan tarde." Hint: "ser".
      5. "Le pedí que [hiciera] la reserva antes del jueves." Hint: "hacer".
      6. "Esperaban que nosotros [llegáramos] antes de medianoche." Hint: "llegar".
    </unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Hear and Write the Imperfect Subjunctive">
      Generate 4 isolated dictation exercises. Each sentence contains at least one imperfect subjunctive verb and is 7-9 words long. The student listens to the full sentence and types the complete transcription, including correct spelling and accent marks. Include a brief English context line for each sentence to orient the listener. Sentences should use a mix of regular and irregular imperfect subjunctive forms.

      Example sentences:
      "Quería que él hablara con el jefe." Context: "A woman describing what she wanted."
      "Era importante que tuviéramos más tiempo." Context: "Someone explaining a past requirement."
      "Me pidieron que fuera a la reunión." Context: "A man describing a request made to him."
      "Dudaba que ella pudiera llegar a tiempo." Context: "Someone expressing a past doubt."
    </unit>

  </section>

  <section name="Hypothetical Si-Clauses: Imperfect Subjunctive + Conditional">

    <unit type="explanation" name="Hypothetical Si-Clauses: Structure and Contrast">
      Explain hypothetical conditional si-clauses in Spanish. Cover the following:

      - The structure: si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional tense
      - Hypothetical si-clauses describe situations that are unlikely, imaginary, or contrary to present reality — things the speaker doesn't expect to happen (or knows aren't currently true)
      - Both word orders work: "Si tuviera dinero, viajaría a España" and "Viajaría a España si tuviera dinero"
      - The conditional tense NEVER directly follows si (reinforce this rule from Section 1)
      - The imperfect subjunctive ONLY appears in the si-clause — the result clause uses the conditional

      **Contrast the two si-clause types clearly:**
      | Type | Si-clause | Result clause | Meaning |
      |------|-----------|---------------|---------|
      | Real | si + present indicative | future / ir + a | plausible, likely condition |
      | Hypothetical | si + imperfect subjunctive | conditional | unlikely, imaginary, or contrary to reality |

      Show 5-6 varied hypothetical si-clause examples using different verbs and subjects. Include at least two paired examples showing the same scenario expressed as real vs. hypothetical to make the contrast vivid (e.g. "Si tienes tiempo, iremos" vs. "Si tuvieras tiempo, iríamos").
    </unit>

    <unit type="conversation" name="Hypothetical Si-Clauses in Dialogue">
      Generate a single conversation (8-10 lines) between two characters — Carmen (32, working in Madrid) and David (34, her friend). They are discussing hypothetical scenarios: what they would do if they had more money, if they didn't have to work, if they lived in a different city. The dialogue should contain at least 5 hypothetical si-clauses using a mix of regular and irregular imperfect subjunctive verbs (tener, poder, vivir, trabajar, ser, hacer). The conversation should feel relaxed and natural — two friends daydreaming about possibilities. Use only the si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional structure.
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Complete the Hypothetical Si-Clause">
      Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a complete hypothetical si-clause sentence with ONE verb blanked — sometimes the verb in the si-clause (imperfect subjunctive), sometimes the verb in the result clause (conditional). Include the infinitive as a hint under each blank. 3 options per blank: the correct form, plus two distractors drawn from: present indicative of the same verb (real si-clause form — the key distractor), future tense of the same verb (wrong for hypothetical), or present subjunctive of the same verb.

      Example exercises:
      1. "Si [tuvieras] más tiempo libre, viajarías mucho." Hint: "tener". Distractors: tienes, tengas.
      2. "Ella [compraría] un piso más grande si ganara más dinero." Hint: "comprar". Distractors: comprará, compra.
      3. "Si yo [pudiera] hablar perfectamente, trabajaría en España." Hint: "poder". Distractors: puedo, pueda.
      4. "Iríamos al concierto si [hubiera] entradas disponibles." Hint: "haber". Distractors: hay, haya.
      5. "Si él [fuera] más organizado, terminaría el proyecto a tiempo." Hint: "ser". Distractors: es, sea.
      6. "¿Qué [harías] si no tuvieras que trabajar?" Hint: "hacer". Distractors: harás, haces.
    </unit>

    <unit type="write_in_blanks" name="Produce the Hypothetical Si-Clause">
      Generate 6 isolated write-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a hypothetical si-clause sentence with TWO blanks — one for the si-clause verb (imperfect subjunctive) and one for the result clause verb (conditional). The student must type both forms from memory. Include the infinitive as a hint under each blank, combined with the subject if there is no explicit subject pronoun in the sentence.

      Example exercises:
      1. "Si yo [tuviera] más dinero, [viajaría] a España." Hints: "tener" for blank 1, "viajar" for blank 2.
      2. "Si [pudiéramos] salir antes, [llegaríamos] a tiempo." Hints: "nosotros - poder" for blank 1, "nosotros - llegar" for blank 2.
      3. "Ella [hablaría] con el director si [supiera] la respuesta." Hints: "hablar" for blank 1, "saber" for blank 2.
      4. "Si [fueras] a Madrid, ¿[visitarías] el Prado?" Hints: "tú - ir" for blank 1, "tú - visitar" for blank 2.
      5. "Si él no [trabajara] tanto, [tendría] más tiempo para su familia." Hints: "trabajar" for blank 1, "tener" for blank 2.
      6. "¿Qué [harías] si [vivieras] en otro país?" Hints: "tú - hacer" for blank 1, "tú - vivir" for blank 2.
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Translate Hypothetical Si-Clauses">
      Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the student to translate into Spanish. All sentences use hypothetical conditional si-clauses — clearly imaginary or contrary-to-fact conditions. Vary subjects, verbs, and whether the si-clause comes first or second. The student must produce both the imperfect subjunctive (in the si-clause) and the conditional (in the result clause).

      Example sentences:
      1. "If I had more time, I would learn guitar."
      2. "She would travel to Argentina if she spoke better Spanish."
      3. "If we didn't have to work, we would live on the coast."
      4. "What would you do if you won a lot of money?"
      5. "If he were here, he would know what to do."
    </unit>

    <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Hypothetical Si-Clause Forms">
      Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is a hypothetical conditional si-clause spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the si-clause verb blanked — always the imperfect subjunctive. The student must identify whether they heard the imperfect subjunctive or a similar-sounding form. 3 options per blank: the correct imperfect subjunctive form, the present subjunctive form (sounds similar but has different ending), and the present indicative form.

      Example exercises:
      Audio: "Si tuvieras más dinero, ¿qué harías?"
      Text: "Si [______] más dinero, ¿qué harías?"
      Options: tuvieras, tengas, tienes.

      Audio: "Si ella pudiera venir, sería perfecto."
      Text: "Si ella [______] venir, sería perfecto."
      Options: pudiera, pueda, puede.

      Audio: "Si viviéramos en España, hablaríamos español todo el día."
      Text: "Si [______] en España, hablaríamos español todo el día."
      Options: viviéramos, vivamos, vivimos.

      Audio: "Yo lo haría si supiera cómo hacerlo."
      Text: "Yo lo haría si [______] cómo hacerlo."
      Options: supiera, sepa, sé.
    </unit>

  </section>

  <review>

    <unit type="explanation" name="Real vs. Hypothetical Si-Clauses: Review Overview">
      Provide a brief recap (no new information) reminding the student of the two si-clause structures covered in this lesson:
      - Real: si + present indicative → future (plausible conditions)
      - Hypothetical: si + imperfect subjunctive → conditional (unlikely/imaginary conditions)
      - Remind them that the conditional NEVER directly follows si
      Frame this as preparation for the mixed exercises ahead where both types appear together and they must discriminate between them.
    </unit>

    <unit type="mcq" name="Real or Hypothetical?">
      Classify each Spanish si-clause sentence as a real conditional or a hypothetical conditional. Explanation: "Decide whether each sentence expresses a real (plausible) condition or a hypothetical (imaginary) one." Generate 8 exercises. Each exercise shows a complete Spanish si-clause sentence. The two options are the same for every exercise: a) Real conditional b) Hypothetical conditional. Mix the sentences roughly 50/50 between the two types. Use varied verbs and subjects.

      Example sentences:
      Real: "Si estudias esta noche, aprobarás el examen mañana."
      Hypothetical: "Si viviera en Madrid, hablaría español todo el día."
      Real: "Si hace buen tiempo, iremos a la playa."
      Hypothetical: "Si tuviera más tiempo, aprendería a tocar el piano."
      Real: "Te ayudaré si me lo pides."
      Hypothetical: "Si pudiera volar, viajaría por todo el mundo."
    </unit>

    <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Mixed Si-Clauses: Choose the Right Form">
      Generate 8 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each is a complete Spanish si-clause sentence with one verb blanked. The mix covers both real and hypothetical si-clauses — approximately 4 of each. The student must identify the si-clause type from context and select the correctly tensed form. Include no hints — the sentence context should make the clause type clear. 4 options per blank spanning all relevant forms: present indicative, future, imperfect subjunctive, and conditional. Each exercise has exactly one correct answer.

      Example exercises:
      1. "Si [tienes] hambre, comeremos algo antes de salir." (real — blank is present indicative) Distractors: tuvieras, tendrás, tendrías.
      2. "Si [tuviera] más paciencia, sería mejor profesora." (hypothetical — blank is imperfect subjunctive) Distractors: tiene, tendrá, tendría.
      3. "Ella [viajará] a Barcelona si consigue el trabajo." (real — blank is future) Distractors: viajara, viajaría, viaja.
      4. "Si [pudiéramos] quedarnos más tiempo, lo haríamos." (hypothetical — blank is imperfect subjunctive) Distractors: podemos, podremos, podríamos.
      5. "Si el vuelo es puntual, [llegaremos] antes de las diez." (real — blank is future) Distractors: llegaríamos, llegáramos, llegamos.
    </unit>

    <unit type="correction" name="Fix the Si-Clause Errors">
      Generate 6 isolated correction exercises. Each is a Spanish si-clause sentence with one or two highlighted errors. Errors should reflect the most common si-clause mistakes: conditional placed directly after si (instead of imperfect subjunctive), future placed in a hypothetical si-clause, present indicative used where imperfect subjunctive is needed, or imperfect subjunctive used in a real si-clause where present indicative belongs. Each incorrect form shown must be a real Spanish word — believable learner errors, not nonsense.

      Example exercises:
      1. "Si ["hablaras": "hablas"] con ella, todo se resolverá." (present indicative used where imperfect subjunctive needed — context is hypothetical)
      2. "Si ["tendrías": "tuvieras"] más dinero, viajarías por Europa." (conditional incorrectly following si)
      3. "Si el tren ["llegara": "llega"] a tiempo, llegaremos antes de las tres." (imperfect subjunctive used in real si-clause)
      4. "Si él ["estudiará": "estudia"] más, aprobará el examen." (future incorrectly in si-clause of real conditional)
      5. "Yo ["iría": "iré"] al parque si el tiempo es bueno." (conditional used in result clause of real si-clause — should be future)
      6. "Si nosotros ["podemos": "pudiéramos"] quedarnos, lo haríamos sin duda." (present indicative used in hypothetical si-clause)
    </unit>

    <unit type="translation" name="Mixed Si-Clause Translation">
      Generate 6 exercises, each a single English sentence for the student to translate into Spanish. The mix covers both real and hypothetical si-clauses — 3 of each, presented in random order. The student must identify the si-clause type from the English meaning and produce the correct structure in full. Vary subjects, verbs, and clause order.

      Example sentences:
      1. "If it rains tomorrow, we won't go out." (real)
      2. "If I could speak Spanish perfectly, I would work in Spain." (hypothetical)
      3. "She will call you if she has news." (real)
      4. "If they had more time, they would visit the whole country." (hypothetical)
      5. "I will help you if you need it." (real)
      6. "What would you do if you lived in another country?" (hypothetical)
    </unit>

    <unit type="writing_practice" name="Your Si-Clause Scenarios">
      Generate a writing prompt asking the student to write a short paragraph (8-10 sentences) about two things: what they will do if their Spanish improves enough to live in Spain (real conditions), and what their life would look like if they already lived there (hypothetical). Objectives:
      1. Include at least 3 real si-clauses using si + present indicative + future.
      2. Include at least 3 hypothetical si-clauses using si + imperfect subjunctive + conditional.
      3. Use at least 2 different irregular imperfect subjunctive verbs (e.g. tuviera, pudiera, fuera, supiera) in the hypothetical si-clauses.
    </unit>

    <unit type="dictation" name="Hear and Transcribe: Mixed Si-Clauses">
      Generate 5 dictation exercises as a cohesive passage — each sentence is part of a short monologue from a Spanish speaker reflecting on their plans and dreams. The passage alternates between real and hypothetical si-clauses. Each sentence is 8-10 words. The student listens and transcribes the full sentence, including correct spelling, accent marks, and punctuation. Include a single English context line for the first sentence only.

      Example sentences:
      "Si consigo el trabajo en Madrid, me mudaré en septiembre." Context: "A man talking about his future plans and dreams."
      "Pero si pudiera elegir, viviría en el sur de España."
      "Si el español me sale bien en la entrevista, tendré muchas posibilidades."
      "Si tuviera más confianza, hablaría con más gente."
      "Y si todo va bien, pronto estaré viviendo mi sueño."
    </unit>

  </review>
</lesson>

lessonTitle: Conditional for Polite Requests and Speculation
lessonOverview:
Lesson Title: Conditional for Polite Requests and Speculation
Description: - Understand conditional use in polite phrasing (¿Me ayudarías? vs. ¿Me ayudas?)
- Practice conditional for speculation about past events (Habría llegado tarde)
- Complete dialogue exercises where characters make polite conditional requests
- Write conversational exchanges using conditional for politeness and speculation
curriculumContext:
Month 2: Advanced Grammar and Conversational Fluency
Month goal: Build confidence with subjunctive mood, conditional structures, and advanced connectors that enable nuanced expression. This month emphasizes practical usage in real conversations and the ability to express doubts, desires, recommendations, and hypothetical scenarios in authentic Spanish.

Previous week (Week 5): Introduction to Subjunctive Mood: Foundations
  Goal: Begin understanding the subjunctive mood by learning when and why it's used in Spanish. This week introduces essential contexts where subjunctive appears, preparing you to recognize and use it in practical conversational situations.
  Lessons:
    - Subjunctive Basics: Why Spanish Uses Subjunctive: Understanding the purpose and logic of the Spanish subjunctive mood and learning to recognize trigger phrases that signal its use.
    - Present Subjunctive Regular Conjugations: Learning how to conjugate regular verbs in the present subjunctive using opposite vowel patterns.
    - Present Subjunctive Irregular Forms and High-Frequency Verbs: - Cover common irregular subjunctive verbs (ser/sea, estar/esté, ir/vaya, tener/tenga, hacer/haga, poder/pueda, saber/sepa)
- Understand that many irregularities follow predictable patterns from present indicative yo-form
- Practice through flashcard-style conjugation exercises
- Translate 10 English sentences containing subjunctive-triggering expressions into Spanish
    - Subjunctive After Verbs of Desire and Emotion: - Learn common verbs triggering subjunctive: querer, desear, esperar, pedir, rogar, preferir, me gusta, temer, sorprender
- Understand the "different subject" requirement (subjunctive appears when main clause subject differs from subordinate clause)
- Complete fill-in-the-blank exercises with emotion and desire verbs
- Write original sentences expressing wishes, preferences, and hopes using subjunctive correctly
    - Introduction to Subjunctive Review: Contexts and Conjugations: - Review when subjunctive is required and how to recognize trigger phrases
- Practice regular and irregular subjunctive conjugations in context
- Complete mixed exercises identifying correct subjunctive forms and appropriate usage
- Write 8-10 sentences expressing desires, hopes, and recommendations using subjunctive mood appropriately

Previous week (Week 6): Subjunctive in Doubt, Negation, and Impersonal Expressions
  Goal: Expand subjunctive usage to expressions of doubt, negation, and impersonal statements. This week covers essential everyday contexts where subjunctive appears in natural Spanish conversation and reasoning.
  Lessons:
    - Subjunctive After Expressions of Doubt and Denial: - Learn subjunctive triggers expressing uncertainty: no creo que, dudo que, es posible que, es probable que, no es seguro que
- Understand that affirmative certainty (creo que, es cierto que) uses indicative, not subjunctive
- Practice with fill-in-the-blank exercises highlighting doubt vs. certainty distinctions
- Rewrite pairs of sentences showing indicative certainty vs. subjunctive doubt
    - Subjunctive in Impersonal Expressions: - Learn impersonal expressions requiring subjunctive: es importante que, es necesario que, es mejor que, conviene que, es extraño que, es lástima que
- Understand that impersonal expressions generally trigger subjunctive (except es verdad que, es obvio que)
- Fill-in-the-blank exercises with impersonal expression triggers
- Write original sentences using impersonal expressions with subjunctive to give advice and express necessity
    - Subjunctive in Recommendations and Advice: - Practice subjunctive after recommendation verbs and impersonal advice phrases
- Understand how subjunctive naturally conveys recommendations (te recomiendo que, te sugiero que, deberías)
- Complete dialogue-based exercises where characters give advice using subjunctive
- Write conversational exchanges where you recommend actions to others using subjunctive
    - Subjunctive in Everyday Conversational Contexts: - Apply subjunctive to realistic social situations: expressing opinions, doubts, desires about daily life
- Practice with scenario-based exercises (workplace, relationships, travel planning)
- Complete fill-in-the-blank in conversational passages using appropriate subjunctive forms
- Write a 200-word dialogue between two people discussing plans, expressing doubts, and making recommendations
    - Subjunctive Doubt, Negation, and Advice Review: - Review subjunctive triggers for doubt, denial, impersonal statements, and recommendations
- Complete comprehensive mixed exercises across all subjunctive contexts learned
- Identify correct subjunctive vs. indicative choices in provided sentences
- Production: Write 300 words expressing your doubts, hopes, recommendations, and concerns about moving to Spain using subjunctive appropriately

>> CURRENT WEEK (Week 7): Conditional Mood and Mixed Tenses
  Goal: Master the conditional mood to express hypothetical situations, polite requests, and speculations. This week teaches you to construct complex sentences combining multiple tenses and moods for sophisticated communication.
  Lessons:
    - Conditional Tense: Formation and Regular Verbs: - Learn conditional formation (infinitive + -ía endings) for regular verbs
- Practice conjugation of regular verbs in conditional (hablaría, comería, viviría)
- Understand conditional use for hypothetical situations and polite requests
- Translate 10 English "would" sentences into Spanish conditional tense
    - Conditional Irregular Verbs and Common Forms: - Cover irregular conditional stems (same as future tense irregulars: tener/tendría, poder/podría, saber/sabría, etc.)
- Practice irregular conditional conjugations through fill-in-the-blank exercises
- Complete sentences showing conditional in polite requests and hypothetical scenarios
- Write sentences expressing what you would do in various hypothetical situations
    - Si (If) Clauses: Real and Hypothetical Conditions: - Learn real conditional structure: present indicative + future/ir + a (Si estudias, aprenderás)
- Learn hypothetical conditional: imperfect subjunctive + conditional (Si estudiaras, aprenderías)
- Practice with fill-in-the-blank exercises showing both conditional types
- Complete and rewrite sentences to practice real vs. hypothetical si-clauses
    - Conditional for Polite Requests and Speculation: - Understand conditional use in polite phrasing (¿Me ayudarías? vs. ¿Me ayudas?)
- Practice conditional for speculation about past events (Habría llegado tarde)
- Complete dialogue exercises where characters make polite conditional requests
- Write conversational exchanges using conditional for politeness and speculation << THIS LESSON
    - Mixed Tenses and Moods Review: Conditional in Context: - Review conditional formation, irregulars, polite usage, and si-clause structures
- Complete comprehensive exercises combining past, present, future, conditional, and subjunctive
- Identify tense/mood choices in complex sentences and explain reasoning
- Production: Write 300 words describing a hypothetical move to Spain, including what you would do, if scenarios, and hopes/recommendations using mixed tenses/moods

Upcoming week (Week 8): Advanced Connectors and Discourse Flow
  Goal: Master sophisticated connectors and discourse markers that enable natural, flowing conversation. This week bridges all grammar learned so far, teaching you to structure longer passages and discussions with professional-level Spanish cohesion.
  Lessons:
    - Contrast, Cause, and Consequence Connectors: - Learn connectors showing contrast: aunque, sin embargo, pero, sino, por el contrario

<plan>

## Step 1: What this lesson actually needs to teach

**Stripping activity suggestions, the genuine learning outcomes are:**

1. **Understand and use the conditional tense for polite requests** — students already know how to *conjugate* the conditional (Lessons 1–2 this week). This is about understanding a specific *use* of a form they already know: that conditional softens requests and questions (¿Me ayudarías? vs ¿Me ayudas?, Me gustaría vs Quiero, ¿Podrías? vs ¿Puedes?).

2. **Form and use the conditional perfect (habría + past participle) for speculation about past events** — "Habría llegado tarde" is the conditional perfect. This is a genuinely *new structure* the student has not seen yet, not just a new use of a known form. The student needs to learn the formation (haber in conditional + past participle) and understand it in speculative contexts.

These are two clearly distinct learning targets: one is a use-case of a known form, the other introduces a brand new structure. Two sections is the right call.

**Curriculum scope check:** Week 7's goal is to "master the conditional mood to express hypothetical situations, polite requests, and speculations" and "construct complex sentences combining multiple tenses." This is Lesson 4 of 5 — the last content lesson before the comprehensive review. It needs to wrap up conditional use cases before the big mixed review. The scope is exactly what the lesson description says.

---

## Step 2 & 3: Sections and unit design

### Section 1: Conditional for Polite Requests

**Section goal:** Produce the conditional tense in realistic conversational scenarios to make polite requests, offers, and questions, understanding why it softens the register compared to present indicative.

Students already know the conjugation. The challenge here is USE: grasping the register distinction, internalising key high-frequency polite forms (¿Me ayudarías?, Me gustaría, ¿Podría/s?, Querría, Debería), and using them in authentic dialogue contexts. Because this is a *use* lesson rather than a *form* lesson, I can move to dialogue and production quickly.

**Units:**
1. **explanation** — Polite conditional use. Cover the contrast with direct requests, key high-frequency structures (me gustaría, ¿podrías?, querría, ¿me ayudarías?, debería), and when/why Spanish speakers use this softening.
2. **conversation** — Two characters in a hotel/shop context using polite conditional requests naturally. Shows the register in real dialogue.
3. **word_match** — Match polite conditional forms to their direct present tense equivalents (¿Me ayudarías? ↔ ¿Me ayudas?, Me gustaría ↔ Quiero, etc.) — quick recall of the register contrast.
4. **fill_in_blanks** — Sentences with the conditional verb blanked; distractors are the equivalent present tense forms. Tests whether the student can pick the polite conditional form when context signals politeness.
5. **listening_fill** — Hear polite conditional vs direct present forms (¿Podría/¿Puede, Me gustaría/Me gusta). The endings are subtle in audio; this trains the ear to notice the politeness distinction.
6. **dialogue_fill** — A customer (Tú) in a hotel/service scenario completing lines with polite conditional forms. Guided production in a conversational context.
7. **translation** — 5 English sentences using "would you…?", "I would like…", "could you…" translated into Spanish conditional. Unscaffolded production.

### Section 2: Conditional Perfect for Speculation

**Section goal:** Form the conditional perfect (habría/habrías/habría/etc. + past participle) and use it to speculate about past events, as well as use the simple conditional for present speculation.

This section introduces a *new structure*. The student needs to learn the formation before they can use it. The conditional perfect uses haber in conditional (habría, habrías, habría, habríamos, habríais, habrían) + past participle — the past participle is known from previous lessons. The speculative use (Habría llegado tarde = He must have arrived late / would have arrived late) is the new semantic function. I'll also briefly contrast with simple conditional used for present speculation (Serían las dos = It must have been around two) since that naturally sets up the perfect.

**Units:**
1. **explanation** — Form of conditional perfect: haber in conditional + past participle. All six forms. Explain speculative use (past events and situations). Brief contrast with simple conditional for present-moment speculation.
2. **conversation** — Two characters speculating about what must have happened to someone/something using conditional perfect. Shows the form in natural use.
3. **mcq** — Classify sentences as using simple conditional (present speculation) vs conditional perfect (past speculation) — tests conceptual discrimination before production.
4. **fill_in_blanks** — Blank the full conditional perfect structure (habría + participle). Hint: infinitive. Distractors: simple conditional form and other person's conditional perfect. Guided production of the new form.
5. **write_in_blanks** — Same type but no options — student must recall and type the conditional perfect form. Hint: infinitive. More demanding production.
6. **dictation** — Hear and transcribe conditional perfect sentences (habría llegado, habríamos salido, etc.). Tests whether students can hear and reproduce the form accurately.
7. **translation** — 5 English "would have / must have" sentences into Spanish conditional perfect. Full unscaffolded production.

### Parallel structure check
Section 1 and Section 2 are NOT parallel — Section 1 is a use-case lesson (form already known, just learning a register function), while Section 2 is a formation lesson (genuinely new structure). The different structures are justified by content, not preference. Section 1 leans heavier on dialogue because it's about register in conversation. Section 2 leans heavier on form-building exercises (mcq for classification, fill then write for the new structure, dictation for audio form recognition).

---

## Step 4: Review

**What the review tests:** Integration of BOTH sections — polite requests (Section 1) and conditional perfect speculation (Section 2) — in the same exercises. The student must discriminate between these two uses of the conditional family and produce both. The review also connects back to si-clauses and earlier conditional knowledge since Lesson 5 will combine all of Week 7 material.

**Units:**
1. **fill_in_blanks** — Cohesive passage (e.g. a letter or WhatsApp-style message to a friend) that mixes polite conditional requests and conditional perfect speculation sentences. Student must pick the correct form from distractors that cross both categories.
2. **correction** — 5–6 sentences with highlighted errors in either polite conditional use (wrong tense used, present tense where conditional needed) or conditional perfect formation (wrong auxiliary, wrong participle, wrong person). Mixed error types.
3. **listening_fill** — Discriminate by ear between simple conditional (querría), present indicative (quiero), and conditional perfect (habría querido). The difference in audio is important as these will all come up in real speech.
4. **dialogue_write** — A conversation scenario where the student uses BOTH polite conditional requests and conditional perfect speculation in their lines. Types responses without options.
5. **live_conversation** — Roleplay at a hotel: check-in and make polite requests using conditional, AND respond to questions about what might have happened (speculate using conditional perfect). Clear end state: check in successfully and complete 3 conversational objectives.

---

## Step 5: Supplementary listening units

- **Section 1:** Added `listening_fill` (unit 5) — polite conditional vs direct present tense discrimination. This is high-value because the register distinction is subtle in audio (¿podría vs ¿puede, me gustaría vs me gusta). ✓
- **Section 2:** Added `dictation` (unit 6) — conditional perfect is phonetically dense (habría + participle). Students need to hear it and reproduce it correctly. ✓
- **Review:** Added `listening_fill` (unit 3) — cross-unit discrimination: simple conditional vs conditional perfect vs present indicative. ✓

Every section has at least one listening or dialogue unit. ✓

---

## Final structure summary

- **Section 1: Conditional for Polite Requests** — 7 units (explanation, conversation, word_match, fill_in_blanks, listening_fill, dialogue_fill, translation)
- **Section 2: Conditional Perfect for Speculation** — 7 units (explanation, conversation, mcq, fill_in_blanks, write_in_blanks, dictation, translation)
- **Review** — 5 units (fill_in_blanks, correction, listening_fill, dialogue_write, live_conversation)

</plan>

<lesson>

<context>This lesson covers two important uses of the conditional: making polite requests and speculating about events. You'll learn how switching from the present indicative to the conditional softens the register of requests and questions, and you'll discover the conditional perfect — a new structure using habría + past participle — which lets you speculate about what would have or must have happened in the past.</context>

<section name="Conditional for Polite Requests">

  <unit type="explanation" name="Softening Requests with the Conditional">
    Explain how the conditional tense is used in Spanish to make requests, questions, and offers sound polite and softened. Cover the core contrast between direct present indicative forms and their polite conditional equivalents: ¿Puedes ayudarme? vs ¿Podrías ayudarme?, Quiero un café vs Querría un café, ¿Me ayudas? vs ¿Me ayudarías?, Me gusta vs Me gustaría. Introduce the key high-frequency verbs used in this way: poder → podría/s, querer → querría, gustar → me/te/le gustaría, deber → debería, hacer → ¿me harías el favor de...? Explain that the conditional creates distance and courtesy — it frames a request as hypothetical rather than a direct demand. Include examples across different social contexts: service interactions (hotel, restaurant), asking a favour from a friend, and making suggestions.
  </unit>

  <unit type="conversation" name="At the Hotel Reception">
    Generate a single conversation between two characters: a hotel guest (Lucía, 30s, polite and friendly) and a hotel receptionist (Marcos, 40s, professional). The conversation should be 8–10 lines and set in a hotel reception. Lucía should use polite conditional forms throughout her lines: me gustaría, ¿podría/s?, ¿me ayudarías?, querría, and ¿sería posible...? Marcos should respond warmly and also use conditional forms when making offers or suggestions. The exchange should feel completely natural and demonstrate how conditional softens a real service interaction.
  </unit>

  <unit type="word_match" name="Polite or Direct?">
    Match each polite conditional phrase to its direct present tense equivalent. Instruction: "Match each polite conditional expression to its more direct present tense equivalent." Left column "Polite Form" contains 5 conditional expressions: ¿Podrías ayudarme?, Me gustaría un café, ¿Me harías un favor?, Querría reservar, ¿Sería posible cambiar? Right column "Direct Form" contains 8 items including correct matches and distractors: ¿Puedes ayudarme?, Quiero un café, ¿Me haces un favor?, Quiero reservar, ¿Es posible cambiar?, ¿Puedo sentarme?, Necesito una habitación, ¿Tienes tiempo?
  </unit>

  <unit type="fill_in_blanks" name="Choose the Polite Form">
    Generate 6 isolated fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each exercise is a Spanish sentence in a polite or formal context. The conditional verb is blanked out. The student selects the correct polite conditional form from options — distractors are the present indicative equivalents of the same verb. No hints — the context of the sentence (polite register, social situation) should make it clear that the conditional is needed. For example:
    1. "Perdone, ¿[podría] indicarme dónde está la estación?" Distractors: puede, podéis, puedo.
    2. "Me [gustaría] pedir una mesa para dos personas, por favor." Distractors: gusta, gustan, gusté.
    3. "¿[Harías] el favor de bajar el volumen un momento?" Distractors: haces, hace, hacéis.
    4. "¿[Podría] usted explicarme cómo funciona esto?" Distractors: puede, pudo, podían.
    5. "[Querría] cambiar esta habitación si es posible." Distractors: quiero, quería, quieren.
    6. "¿[Me ayudarías] a llevar estas maletas, por favor?" Distractors: me ayudas, me ayudé, me ayudaban.
  </unit>

  <unit type="listening_fill" name="Hear the Politeness">
    Generate 5 listening fill-in-the-blank exercises. Each sentence is spoken in full via audio. The text displays the sentence with the verb blanked out. The student selects which form they heard from 3 options. All options are forms of the SAME verb — one is the polite conditional, one is the present indicative, and one is another conditional or imperfect form — so the student must discriminate the tense purely by ear. For example:
    Audio: "¿Podrías traerme la carta, por favor?" Text: "¿[______] traerme la carta, por favor?" Options: podrías, puedes, podías.
    Audio: "Me gustaría reservar una mesa para esta noche." Text: "Me [______] reservar una mesa para esta noche." Options: gustaría, gusta, gustaba.
    Audio: "¿Me ayudarías a encontrar la calle Mayor?" Text: "¿Me [______] a encontrar la calle Mayor?" Options: ayudarías, ayudas, ayudabas.
    Audio: "Querría un café solo y un croissant, por favor." Text: "[______] un café solo y un croissant, por favor." Options: querría, quiero, quería.
    Audio: "¿Haría el favor de repetir eso más despacio?" Text: "¿[______] el favor de repetir eso más despacio?" Options: haría, hace, hizo.
  </unit>

  <unit type="dialogue_fill" name="Checking In at the Hotel">
    Generate a dialogue completion exercise. The user (Tú) is a guest checking into a Spanish hotel. The other character is a receptionist (Recepcionista, female). The dialogue is 7 turns total (4 for the receptionist, 3 for the user). The user's turns have the conditional verb (or conditional expression) blanked out. No hints — the context and the receptionist's lines make the expected polite form clear. 2 distractors per blank using the direct present indicative of the same verb. For example:

  </unit>

  <unit type="translation" name="Translate Polite Requests">
    Generate 5 exercises, each a single English sentence for the user to translate into Spanish using the conditional for polite phrasing. The sentences should cover a range of high-frequency polite structures: me gustaría, ¿podrías/podría?, ¿me ayudarías?, querría, ¿sería posible? For example:
    1. "I would like a room with a view, please."
    2. "Could you (tú) help me with my suitcase?"
    3. "Would it be possible to change the reservation?"

    5. "I would like to know what time the restaurant opens."
  </unit>

</section>

<section name="Conditional Perfect for Speculation">

  <unit type="explanation" name="Habría + Past Participle: Speculating About the Past">
    Introduce the conditional perfect tense as a new structure. Explain formation: haber conjugated in the conditional (habría, habrías, habría, habríamos, habríais, habrían) + past participle of the main verb (the same past participles students already know: hablado, comido, vivido, tenido, hecho, sido, ido, etc.). Show all six forms in a clear table using the verb llegar. Explain that the conditional perfect has two key uses: (1) saying what would have happened in a hypothetical scenario (Si hubieras llamado, lo habría sabido — though this combination is for later), and (2) speculating about what must have happened in the past (Habría llegado tarde = He must have arrived late / He would have arrived late). Briefly contrast with the simple conditional used for present-moment speculation (¿Dónde estarán? → ¿Dónde estarían? = I wonder where they were / they must have been somewhere). Make clear that the conditional perfect refers to completed past events, while the simple conditional refers to situations around the time being described. Include 4–5 clear speculative example sentences: Habría perdido el tren. ¿Lo habría sabido ya? No habría llegado a tiempo. Habrían salido antes.
  </unit>

  <unit type="conversation" name="What Could Have Happened?">
    Generate a single conversation between two characters: Elena (late 20s, curious and expressive) and her colleague David (early 30s, thoughtful). Both characters should use conditional perfect forms to speculate: Habría tenido un problema, Se habría olvidado, ¿Le habría pasado algo?, No habría visto el mensaje. Mix sentences clearly — some should use simple conditional (Sería tarde cuando llegaste), some should use conditional perfect (Habrían salido ya, ¿Lo habría sabido él?). Include both first and third person examples. For example:
    1. "Habría llegado al aeropuerto sobre las diez." → conditional perfect
    2. "Con más tiempo, lo haría mejor." → simple conditional
    3. "¿Dónde estarían cuando llamé?" → simple conditional
    4. "No habrían visto el mensaje todavía." → conditional perfect
  </unit>

Each exercise is a Spanish sentence where the conditional perfect is blanked out as a unit (both the auxiliary habría/etc. and the past participle together form the blank).     Audio: "Habría perdido el vuelo sin duda." Text: "[______] el vuelo sin duda." Options: habría perdido, perdería, pierde.
    Audio: "¿Podría usted repetir eso, por favor?" Text: "¿[______] usted repetir eso, por favor?" Options: podría, puede, habría podido.
    Audio: "Ellos habrían llegado antes si no había tráfico." Text: "Ellos [______] antes si no había tráfico." Options: habrían llegado, llegarían, llegaron.
  </unit>

  <unit type="dialogue_write" name="Making Plans and Speculating">
    Generate a dialogue completion exercise. The user (Tú) is chatting with a Spanish friend (Carla, late 20s, chatty and direct) about a party they both missed last week and about upcoming plans. The dialogue is 8 turns total (4 for Carla, 4 for the user). The user's turns have blanks requiring BOTH types of conditional: polite conditional for making requests/suggestions about upcoming plans, and conditional perfect for speculating about what happened at the party. Each user turn has 1 blank. Include infinitive hints for conditional perfect blanks; no hints for polite conditional blanks where context makes the form clear. Maximum 4 blanks total across the dialogue. For example:

    Carla: ¡No puedo creer que no fuimos a la fiesta de Ana! ¿Qué crees que pasó?
    Tú: No sé, [habría habido] muchísima gente, seguramente. Hint: "haber".
    Carla: ¡Exacto! Y seguro que se acabó la comida pronto, ¿no?
    Tú: Sí, con tanta gente la comida [se habría terminado] en un momento. Hint: "terminarse".
    Carla: Oye, ¿quedamos este fin de semana para compensar?
    Tú: ¡Sí! [Me gustaría] ir a ese nuevo bar del centro. No hint.
    Carla: Perfecto. ¿A qué hora quedamos?
    Tú: ¿[Podrías] quedar sobre las ocho? No hint.
  </unit>

  <unit type="live_conversation" name="Hotel Check-In and Speculation">
    Generate a roleplay scene set in a hotel lobby in Madrid. The user is a guest who has just arrived. The character is Sofía (35, warm and efficient hotel receptionist). The main scenario is completing the hotel check-in and leaving the lobby — a clear closed-loop end state. Objectives:
    1. Make at least two polite requests using the conditional (e.g. ask about Wi-Fi, request a specific room type, ask for a dinner recommendation).
    2. Speculate using the conditional perfect about what happened to a piece of luggage that hasn't arrived yet (e.g. Habría quedado en el aeropuerto...).
    3. Ask Sofía if she would be able to help arrange a taxi for the following morning using a polite conditional.
  </unit>

</review>

</lesson>
